title: Replace per-tree Mesh draw calls with InstancedMesh in a forestry carbon-mapping viewer
business_context: A carbon-credit verification platform renders satellite-derived tree canopy maps for auditors. A single 10km x 10km parcel contains up to 240,000 individual tree markers, each rendered as its own THREE.Mesh. On mid-range laptops the scene drops to 6 FPS and auditors abandon sessions; the support queue shows 38% of parcel reviews timing out. We bill $1.2M/yr and the renderer is the #1 churn reason.
constraints: Must keep per-tree click selection (raycast) and per-tree color by carbon class
Up to 240k instances per parcel, target 60 FPS on Intel Iris Xe
Backward-compat: existing GeoJSON tile API and tree IDs must not change
No more than 80MB GPU memory budget per parcel
Selection highlight must update within one frame without rebuilding buffers

before_state.description: Each tree is an individual Mesh added to the scene; 240k draw calls per frame plus 240k matrix updates on the CPU.

issues: 240k separate draw calls — GPU submission alone exceeds the 16ms frame budget
this.mat.clone() per tree creates 240k materials, blowing the GPU memory budget
intersectObjects over 240k meshes does a linear test per pick, freezing the UI on click
No frustum or LOD culling; off-screen trees still cost CPU matrix updates

reasoning: Naive approach: keep individual Meshes but enable frustum culling and hope it helps. Rejected — culling only removes off-screen draws; a zoomed-out parcel still shows all 240k on screen, so the worst case is unchanged. Alternative considered: THREE.Points / sprites. Rejected because auditors need real 3D canopy shape and per-tree shadows, which points cannot give, and point picking is imprecise at overlap. Chosen path: a single InstancedMesh with per-instance matrices in an InstancedBufferAttribute and per-instance color via setColorAt. Cross-layer trace: UI raycaster.intersectObject(instancedMesh) returns instanceId -> map instanceId to tree.id via a typed Uint32 index built at load -> color updates write into the existing instanceColor buffer with a single needsUpdate flag instead of touching 240k materials -> the GeoJSON API and IDs are untouched; only the render layer changes -> a GPU-side LOD swaps to a billboard geometry beyond 2km -> tests assert instance count, picked id, and that selecting updates exactly one color entry; rollout behind a renderer flag with FPS telemetry.

after_state.description: One InstancedMesh holds all trees; matrices and colors live in GPU buffers, picking uses instanceId, LOD swaps geometry by distance.

files.purpose: Collapses 240k draw calls into one InstancedMesh and makes picking O(1) via instanceId->id mapping.
Provides distance-based geometry selection with a hysteresis band so the LOD does not thrash at the swap boundary.
Drives the render loop and emits FPS telemetry used to gate the rollout flag.

migration_strategy: Add InstancedMesh path behind renderer flag tree_instancing=off; both renderers consume the same GeoJSON tiles
Canary on 5% of parcels under 50k trees; compare FPS telemetry and pick-accuracy diffs against the mesh renderer
Ramp to all parcels under 100k, then enable LOD billboard swap and re-measure GPU memory
Flip default to instancing for all parcels; keep mesh path one release for rollback
Delete TreeLayer mesh-array code and per-tree material clones once two weeks of telemetry show no regressions
testing_strategy: Fixture: a deterministic 50k-tree parcel JSON with known IDs and carbon classes. Assert output: instance count == 50k, raycast at a known screen coord returns the expected tree.id, and highlight(id) mutates exactly one entry of instanceColor (diff the buffer before/after). Side-effect assert: only one draw call recorded via renderer.info.render.calls == 1 for the layer. Chaos test: feed a 240k parcel with 3% NaN coordinates and assert load() skips them without throwing and FPS telemetry stays >=55 over 120 frames.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At 240k individual Meshes the per-frame draw-call submission and CPU matrix updates exceed the 16ms budget before any pixels are shaded, so the scene is GPU-bound on submission overhead, not fill rate — instancing is the only fix that removes the per-object submission cost.

title: Stream a 9GB LiDAR point cloud into a WebGPU viewer with octree-based progressive loading
business_context: A surveying SaaS lets civil engineers inspect drone LiDAR scans of construction sites. Scans are 400M+ points (9GB raw). The current viewer downloads the full .las before rendering, so first paint takes 3-7 minutes and 22% of sessions are abandoned before anything appears. Engineers need to spin the model within 5 seconds to judge cut/fill volumes on-site over LTE.
constraints: First meaningful render within 5s on a 20Mbps connection
Support 400M points without exceeding 1.5GB GPU memory
Must run on WebGPU with a WebGL2 fallback path for Safari < 18
Progressive refinement: never block interaction while loading
Point IDs stable so measurement annotations survive reload

before_state.description: Monolithic loader fetches the entire .las, parses on the main thread, then uploads one giant buffer.

issues: Downloads all 9GB before first paint — multi-minute blank screen
Parses 400M points synchronously on the main thread, freezing the tab
Single Float32Array of 4.8GB exceeds the 32-bit TypedArray and GPU limits
No spatial structure, so no culling or level-of-detail possible

reasoning: Naive approach: chunk the fetch with HTTP Range requests but still upload everything. Rejected — it removes the blank screen but still tries to hold 4.8GB of positions in one buffer, which exceeds both the TypedArray 2^31-byte cap and the GPU budget. Alternative: decimate to 5M points server-side and serve a single low-res cloud. Rejected because engineers measure sub-centimeter cut/fill and need full density on zoom. Chosen path: pre-bake a Potree-style octree of nodes server-side; the client loads root + visible nodes by frustum and screen-space error. Cross-layer trace: UI camera frustum -> a traversal picks octree node IDs whose projected size exceeds 1px -> a Web Worker fetches and decodes those node binaries off-thread -> decoded chunks upload as separate GPUBuffers indexed by node ID -> annotations key off stable point IDs encoded in node files -> WebGPU render pipeline draws visible node buffers; WebGL2 fallback uses gl.POINTS -> tests assert node selection by camera, worker decode correctness, and memory cap.

after_state.description: Octree streaming: only frustum-visible, screen-relevant nodes are fetched and decoded in workers, capped by a memory budget with LRU eviction.

files.purpose: Frustum + screen-space-error traversal that yields only the node IDs worth loading within a point budget.
Parses node binaries in a worker and transfers buffers (zero-copy) so the main thread never blocks.
Manages GPU buffers per node with an LRU eviction loop that enforces the 1.5GB memory cap.
Keeps Safari working by drawing the same per-node buffers via WebGL2 gl.POINTS when WebGPU is unavailable.

migration_strategy: Deploy server-side octree baker; emit node tree alongside legacy .las so old viewer still works
Ship new viewer behind flag cloud_streaming=off; route 10% of opens to it, measure time-to-first-render
Enable WebGPU path where navigator.gpu exists, WebGL2 fallback elsewhere; verify annotation IDs round-trip
Ramp to 100%; keep monolithic loader for one release as rollback
Decommission full-.las download endpoint after 30 days of zero fallback hits
testing_strategy: Fixture: a baked 12-node octree with known point counts and IDs. Assert: visibleNodes for a camera looking at the root returns root + the 4 nearest children and never exceeds the budget; worker decode of node A yields the exact known positions/IDs. Side-effect: NodeBuffers.upload past the cap evicts the least-recently-used node and total bytes stays <= cap. Chaos test: simulate a node fetch returning HTTP 500 mid-stream and assert the traversal skips it, keeps rendering loaded nodes, and retries with backoff without leaking a GPUBuffer.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single 4.8GB Float32Array cannot exist — it exceeds the 2^31-byte TypedArray cap — and even chunked it cannot be uploaded as one GPU buffer; only spatial subdivision with per-node buffers stays within the device limits.

title: Fix WebGL context-loss crashes in a long-running trading-floor 3D order-book heatmap
business_context: A fintech desk runs a 3D depth-of-market heatmap on wall displays 24/7. After 6-10 hours the GPU driver issues a context-loss (driver reset / TDR) and the canvas goes black until someone reloads, losing the live book view during volatile sessions. Trading ops logged 14 black-screen incidents last month, each a 2-4 minute blind spot on a desk doing $400M/day in flow.
constraints: Must auto-recover from webglcontextlost within 2s with no manual reload
No loss of the live order-book subscription during recovery
Recovery must re-upload all GPU resources (textures, buffers, shaders)
Cannot drop more than 1 book snapshot during recovery
Runs unattended for weeks; zero memory growth across recovery cycles

before_state.description: Renderer assumes the WebGL context never dies; resources are created once at startup and never re-created.

issues: No webglcontextlost / webglcontextrestored listeners — a driver reset leaves a dead context forever
gl.getContext returns a context with all resources invalidated after loss; draw() then silently no-ops or throws
No abstraction to re-create textures/programs, so recovery would require a full app restart
Book subscription lives in the same object and would be torn down on reload

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap draw() in try/catch and reload the page on error. Rejected — a reload drops the websocket book subscription and shows a multi-second blank, exactly the incident we are eliminating. Alternative: switch to a worker + OffscreenCanvas so a crash is isolated. Rejected as a larger rewrite that still needs context-restore handling and does not by itself survive a driver TDR. Chosen path: listen for webglcontextlost (preventDefault to allow restore) and webglcontextrestored, and put all GPU resource creation behind an idempotent buildResources() that recovery re-runs. Cross-layer trace: the canvas fires webglcontextlost -> we preventDefault and flip a 'lost' flag that pauses draw but keeps the websocket alive and buffering the latest snapshot -> on webglcontextrestored we call buildResources() to recreate program/textures/buffers -> the buffered latest book is re-uploaded so at most the in-flight frame is lost -> a watchdog forces context restore via loseContext extension if no restore arrives in 2s -> tests simulate loss/restore and assert resources are rebuilt and no snapshot beyond the in-flight one is dropped.

after_state.description: Context-loss-aware renderer: pauses on loss, rebuilds all GPU state on restore, keeps the data subscription alive and replays the latest buffered snapshot.

files.purpose: Adds context-lost/restored handlers and an idempotent buildResources() so the renderer survives driver resets and replays the latest book.
Bounds recovery time to 2s by forcing a context restore when the driver does not auto-restore.
Decouples the data subscription from the GPU lifecycle so a context loss never tears down the live feed.

migration_strategy: Add context-loss handlers and buildResources() behind flag ctx_recovery=off; default still old renderer
Force-trigger loss in staging via WEBGL_lose_context and verify auto-recovery + book replay
Enable on 2 non-critical wall displays for 72h; monitor recovery count and memory
Roll out to all displays; keep watchdog disabled then enable after 1 week of clean restores
Remove the old reload-on-error path once 30 days pass with zero manual reloads
testing_strategy: Fixture: headless renderer with a stubbed gl that can flip isContextLost(). Assert output: after dispatching webglcontextlost then webglcontextrestored, prog and tex are non-null new objects and the last submitted book is re-drawn (spy on draw). Side-effect: BookFeed websocket onmessage count is unaffected across a loss cycle (subscription not torn down). Chaos test: fire 500 loss/restore cycles in a loop and assert no growth in created textures/programs (each restore destroys/replaces) and heap stays flat.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reloading the page on a context loss tears down the websocket and shows a multi-second blank during the most volatile moments — exactly the blind spot being eliminated — whereas in-place resource rebuild keeps the live feed and recovers within 2s.

title: Batch 12k IoT sensor markers into a merged BufferGeometry with per-vertex status colors
business_context: A smart-building IoT dashboard renders a 3D digital twin of a 40-floor tower with 12,000 live sensors (temp, CO2, occupancy). Each sensor was a separate React-Three-Fiber mesh re-created on every state poll (every 5s), causing a 900ms GC pause and dropped frames each cycle. Facilities managers reported the twin 'stuttering' during alarms when it matters most.
constraints: 12k sensors, status updates every 5s without re-allocating geometry
Per-sensor color must reflect live status (ok/warn/alarm) with sub-frame update
Backward-compat with existing sensor IDs and floor metadata
React-Three-Fiber app; cannot block the React render thread
Memory must stay flat across 1000 update cycles (no GC sawtooth)

before_state.description: Each poll maps sensors to JSX meshes, so React reconciles and three.js re-creates 12k meshes every 5s.

issues: 12k <mesh> elements reconciled and rebuilt every 5s -> 900ms GC pause
sphereGeometry recreated per sensor per poll instead of shared
New array identity each poll forces full remount even for unchanged sensors
Color change recreates the whole mesh tree instead of mutating a buffer

reasoning: Naive approach: memoize each <mesh> by id so unchanged sensors skip re-render. Rejected — even memoized, 12k React fibers still cost reconciliation time and the geometry/material churn remains on status flips. Alternative: InstancedMesh with setColorAt. Rejected here only because the team also needs per-vertex gradient shading across a merged floor mesh for heat overlays, which instancing's flat per-instance color cannot express. Chosen path: one merged BufferGeometry built once with a per-vertex color attribute; status polls write into the existing color attribute and set needsUpdate. Cross-layer trace: sensor poll updates a Map<id,status> outside React -> a useFrame hook diffs changed IDs and writes their vertices' RGB into the shared Float32 color attribute -> needsUpdate=true uploads only the changed range -> React renders one <mesh> with the merged geometry, never remounting -> IDs/floor metadata preserved in a parallel index array -> tests assert color buffer mutation and flat allocation.

after_state.description: One merged geometry built once; live status writes into its color attribute via useFrame with no remounts.

files.purpose: Builds the merged geometry once and mutates only changed vertices' colors per frame, eliminating remounts.
Holds live status outside React and tracks a dirty set so only changed sensors are repainted.
Feeds the out-of-React store every 5s without touching the component tree.

migration_strategy: Introduce merged-geometry component behind flag twin_merged=off alongside the per-mesh version
Canary on two floors; compare frame-time during simulated alarm storms
Move status into the out-of-React store and verify dirty-only updates
Enable globally; keep per-mesh component one release for rollback
Delete per-mesh path after a week of flat GC telemetry
testing_strategy: Fixture: 12k-sensor layout with deterministic positions. Assert: after sensorStore.apply flips 30 sensors to 'alarm', exactly 30 vertices' color triplets change to red and needsUpdate fires once. Side-effect: number of THREE.BufferGeometry instances created stays 1 across 1000 polls (spy on constructor). Chaos test: push a malformed poll with an unknown sensor id and assert it is ignored without throwing and the dirty set is cleared.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Re-mapping 12k sensors to JSX meshes every poll forces React to reconcile 12k fibers and three.js to rebuild geometries, producing a 900ms GC pause precisely during alarm bursts when smooth updates matter most.

title: Move heavy delaunay terrain triangulation off the main thread into a SharedArrayBuffer worker pool
business_context: A logistics company visualizes warehouse drone-scanned floor elevation as a 3D mesh to plan AGV routes. Re-triangulating 2M scan points after each re-scan blocks the main thread for 4.2s, freezing the planning UI and the live AGV telemetry overlay. Planners run 30+ re-scans per shift; the freeze compounds into ~20 lost minutes/planner/day.
constraints: 2M points triangulated without blocking the UI > 50ms at a time
Result mesh must update the existing Three.js geometry in place
Use SharedArrayBuffer so workers avoid copying 24MB per pass
COOP/COEP headers required; must not break existing embeds
AGV telemetry overlay must keep rendering at 60 FPS during triangulation

before_state.description: Triangulation runs synchronously on the main thread on every re-scan.

issues: Delaunator.from on 2M points blocks the main thread ~4.2s
computeVertexNormals adds another synchronous pass over millions of tris
Array.from(d.triangles) allocates a huge JS array, spiking GC
Whole UI including AGV telemetry freezes during every re-scan

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap buildTerrain in requestIdleCallback so it runs 'when idle'. Rejected — the triangulation is one indivisible call; idle scheduling just delays the same 4.2s freeze, it does not break it up. Alternative: a single dedicated worker. Rejected because copying the 24MB point buffer in and the index buffer out via postMessage adds ~300ms of structured-clone per pass and still serializes normal computation. Chosen path: a SharedArrayBuffer holding points + output index, a small worker pool that triangulates and computes normals into the shared buffer, and the main thread only swaps the geometry's attributes. Cross-layer trace: planner triggers re-scan -> points written into a SharedArrayBuffer -> a worker runs Delaunator over the shared view and writes triangles+normals back into shared memory (no copy) -> main thread receives a 'done' signal and points the existing BufferGeometry at the shared buffers, calling setIndex/needsUpdate -> COOP/COEP headers enable SAB -> AGV overlay keeps rendering since the main thread was never blocked -> tests assert triangle count and that the main thread stayed responsive.

after_state.description: Triangulation and normal computation run in a worker over a SharedArrayBuffer; the main thread only swaps attribute references.

files.purpose: Owns the worker and SharedArrayBuffers; the main thread only swaps geometry attributes after the worker signals done.
Runs Delaunator over the shared point view and writes triangle indices back into shared memory with an atomic count.
Sets COOP/COEP so SharedArrayBuffer is available in the browser.

files.content:
// AGV telemetry overlay renders independently; proves the main thread stays free during builds.
export function startOverlay(render: () => void){ const loop=()=>{ render(); requestAnimationFrame(loop); }; requestAnimationFrame(loop); }
files.purpose: Demonstrates the telemetry overlay keeps its 60 FPS loop while triangulation runs off-thread.

migration_strategy: Add COOP/COEP headers; verify existing embeds still load (some may need CORP on assets)
Ship worker builder behind flag terrain_worker=off; run both and diff triangle counts
Canary on one warehouse; measure main-thread block time during re-scans
Enable globally once block time < 50ms and overlay holds 60 FPS
Remove synchronous buildTerrain after two clean weeks
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 100k-point scan with a known Delaunay triangle count. Assert: worker output triangle count matches the reference and the geometry index updates in place (same BufferGeometry instance). Side-effect: main-thread long-task observer records no task > 50ms during a build. Chaos test: send two re-scans back-to-back and assert the second supersedes the first without corrupting the shared index buffer (atomic count consistent).
what_breaks_naive_approach: requestIdleCallback cannot subdivide a single 4.2s Delaunator call, so the main thread still freezes in one block and the AGV telemetry overlay stalls during every re-scan.

title: Eliminate texture-atlas bleeding in a 3D product configurator's instanced decal layer
business_context: An e-commerce furniture configurator lets shoppers apply fabric swatches to a 3D sofa in real time. Swatches are packed into a 4096x4096 texture atlas and applied via instanced decals. At certain camera angles and mip levels, neighboring swatches bleed in as 1-2px colored seams, so a navy sofa shows pink edges. Conversion analytics tie a 4% configurator bounce to 'looks broken' feedback.
constraints: No visible seam at any mip level or anisotropy setting
Keep a single atlas draw (cannot split into N textures, GPU bind limit)
Support 380 swatches in one 4096 atlas
Must work on iOS Safari (no seamless cubemap tricks)
Decal layer stays instanced; no per-swatch material

before_state.description: UVs index directly into atlas cells with no padding; mipmapping samples across cell borders.

issues: UVs use exact cell edges, so bilinear/mip sampling pulls in neighbor texels
No gutter/padding between cells in the packed atlas
generateMipmaps blends across cell boundaries at coarse mips
Anisotropic filtering widens the bleed at grazing angles

reasoning: Naive approach: clamp UVs with a tiny epsilon inset (u0+0.001). Rejected — a fixed epsilon does not scale across mip levels; at mip 4 the footprint is 16 texels wide and a 0.001 inset still bleeds. Alternative: disable mipmaps to stop cross-cell blending. Rejected because it produces shimmering aliasing on the moving sofa and tanks far-view quality. Chosen path: re-bake the atlas with a per-cell gutter (extruded edge padding) so cells are 256 content + 8px bleed border, generate mipmaps only down to the level where a cell still spans >=2 texels, and inset UVs by half a texel. Cross-layer trace: build step re-packs swatches with edge-extruded gutters -> atlas metadata records content rect inside the padded cell -> uvForSwatch returns the inset content rect -> texture is created with a capped maxMipLevel and CLAMP wrapping -> instanced decal shader samples the padded atlas -> tests render a known cell at multiple mips and assert no neighbor color appears in the border.

after_state.description: Atlas re-baked with gutters; UVs inset by half a texel; mip levels capped so cross-cell bleed cannot occur.

files.purpose: Computes UVs into the gutter-padded content rect with a half-texel inset so no sample reaches a neighbor cell.
Bakes the atlas with edge-extruded gutters so mip downsampling stays within each swatch's color.
Creates the atlas texture with clamped wrapping and a capped mip level so coarse mips never blend across cells.

migration_strategy: Re-bake atlas with gutters as a new versioned asset atlas_v2.png; keep v1 served
Ship uvForSwatch + texture changes behind flag atlas_gutter=off reading atlas version
Canary: render diff a fixed set of swatches/angles vs v1 and flag any non-content pixel in borders
Ramp to 100% after seam tests pass on iOS Safari device lab
Remove atlas_v1 and old UV math after one release
testing_strategy: Fixture: a synthetic atlas where each cell is a solid unique color. Assert: sampling the rendered decal at cell border pixels across mip 0-4 returns only that cell's color (no neighbor color within tolerance). Side-effect: only one texture bound (renderer.info.memory.textures == 1 for the layer). Chaos test: request a swatch index beyond the atlas range and assert uvForSwatch clamps to the last valid cell rather than producing out-of-atlas UVs.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A fixed epsilon UV inset is defined in normalized space but bleed happens in texel space that grows with mip level, so at coarse mips a 16-texel footprint still straddles the neighbor and the navy sofa keeps its pink seam.

title: Build a frame-budget-aware scheduler to keep a medical 3D anatomy viewer at 60 FPS
business_context: A healthcare ed-tech platform renders interactive 3D anatomy (heart, vasculature) for med students. The viewer does too much per frame: label layout, raycasting for hover, animation, and texture decode all run unconditionally, so the frame time swings 9-34ms and the model judders during rotation. Instructors report students getting motion sick in VR mode, where dropped frames are unacceptable.
constraints: Hold a 16.6ms frame budget (90 FPS in VR = 11ms)
Defer non-critical work (labels, hover) when over budget
Hover raycast must still feel responsive (< 100ms latency)
Animation and camera must never be deferred
Deterministic ordering so deferral never starves a task forever

before_state.description: Every subsystem runs every frame regardless of remaining budget.

issues: layoutLabels + texture decode can blow the budget in the same frame as a texture arrival
No notion of remaining time; work is unconditional
Hover and labels compete with critical animation for the frame
Texture decode is unbounded — a batch arrival causes a 10ms+ spike

reasoning: Naive approach: throttle each subsystem to every Nth frame. Rejected — fixed throttling either starves hover (feels laggy) or still collides when a texture batch lands on a label-layout frame. Alternative: move everything to a worker. Rejected because label layout needs DOM/camera projection on the main thread and hover raycast needs the live scene graph. Chosen path: a priority task queue with a per-frame time budget; critical tasks (animation, render) always run, deferred tasks run only while time remains, and a starvation guard force-runs any task skipped N frames. Cross-layer trace: requestAnimationFrame gives a timestamp -> scheduler computes remaining budget after critical work -> it drains the priority queue (hover > labels > texture-decode) until budget exhausted, slicing texture decode into bounded chunks -> skipped tasks increment a starvation counter that forces them in once it hits a cap -> render always runs last -> VR path uses an 11ms budget -> tests assert budget adherence and no starvation.

after_state.description: A frame scheduler runs critical work first, then drains deferred tasks within the remaining budget, with starvation protection.

files.purpose: Runs critical work, then drains deferred tasks only while budget remains, forcing starved tasks after N skips.
Wires subsystems into the scheduler with priorities and lets the budget govern deferred work.
Slices texture decode into budget-bounded chunks so a batch arrival cannot spike one frame.

migration_strategy: Introduce scheduler behind flag frame_budget=off; default keeps unconditional loop
Canary in desktop viewer; record frame-time histogram before/after
Enable VR 11ms budget path and validate no judder in headset QA
Roll out to all; keep old loop for one release
Remove unconditional loop after frame-time p99 < 16ms sustained
testing_strategy: Fixture: a synthetic frame where critical work costs 8ms and three deferred tasks cost 3/6/4ms with a 16.6ms budget. Assert: hover runs every frame, labels run only when budget allows, and total frame time stays <= budget except when a starved task is forced. Side-effect: a task skipped 5 frames in a row is force-run on the 6th (assert skips counter resets). Chaos test: inject a critical task that itself takes 20ms and assert the scheduler still runs render and resets next frame without deadlock.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Fixed per-Nth-frame throttling cannot react to a texture batch landing on the same frame as label layout, so the combined cost still blows the budget and the VR view drops frames, causing motion sickness.

title: Convert a GPU-particle weather radar layer from CPU vertex updates to a transform-feedback simulation
business_context: A weather app renders animated precipitation as 500k GPU particles over a 3D globe. Today positions are advanced on the CPU each frame and re-uploaded, costing 11ms/frame just on the upload and stalling the pipeline. During severe-weather events traffic spikes 8x and the map stutters exactly when users need it. The product owner wants smooth 60 FPS on a mid-tier Android phone.
constraints: 500k particles advanced entirely on GPU; no per-frame CPU upload
Deterministic wind-field advection (same seed -> same motion)
WebGL2 transform feedback (no compute shaders on target devices)
Particles respawn at storm cells without CPU round-trip
Keep existing globe shader and lighting untouched

before_state.description: CPU integrates particle positions each frame and re-uploads the whole vertex buffer.

issues: bufferData re-uploads 6MB every frame -> ~11ms PCIe/driver cost
CPU loop over 1.5M floats stalls the main thread
Respawn logic on CPU forces the upload even when little changed
No way to sample a GPU wind-field texture for advection

reasoning: Naive approach: only upload the sub-range that changed via bufferSubData. Rejected — essentially all particles move each frame, so the sub-range is the whole buffer; no savings. Alternative: WebGL2/WebGPU compute shader. Rejected because target Android devices lack reliable compute support, but they do support WebGL2 transform feedback. Chosen path: a transform-feedback pass where a vertex shader reads current position/velocity, samples a wind-field texture, integrates, and writes new positions into a second buffer; ping-pong the two buffers so nothing leaves the GPU. Cross-layer trace: a wind-field texture is uploaded once (not per frame) -> the TF program's vertex shader advects each particle and handles respawn in GLSL -> outputs capture into buffer B while reading buffer A -> swap A/B each frame -> the render pass draws the current buffer with the unchanged globe shader -> determinism via a seeded hash in-shader -> tests assert positions evolve identically to a CPU reference for a few steps.

after_state.description: Transform-feedback advection runs entirely on the GPU with ping-pong buffers; CPU only swaps buffer bindings.

files.purpose: Runs the advection as a transform-feedback pass and ping-pongs buffers so positions never leave the GPU.
GPU vertex shader that samples the wind field, integrates position, and deterministically respawns particles.
Builds and links the transform-feedback program, declaring the captured varying before link.

migration_strategy: Add TF sim behind flag gpu_particles=off; CPU path remains default
Verify TF positions match CPU reference for the first 30 steps on a fixed wind field
Canary on Android mid-tier devices; measure frame time during a synthetic storm spike
Enable globally; keep CPU integrator for devices lacking WebGL2 TF
Delete CPU per-frame upload after a release of clean telemetry
testing_strategy: Fixture: a deterministic 64x64 wind texture and 1000 seeded particles. Assert: after 10 TF steps the read-back positions match a CPU reference within float tolerance. Side-effect: no bufferData call occurs during step() (spy confirms zero per-frame uploads). Chaos test: bind a wind texture that is not yet loaded (1x1 placeholder) and assert the sim still advances without NaNs and respawn keeps particles in-bounds.
what_breaks_naive_approach: bufferSubData saves nothing because virtually every particle moves each frame, so the 6MB/frame upload cost remains and the map keeps stuttering during the 8x storm spike.

title: Add depth-sorted OIT for transparent 3D buildings in a city planning Mapbox-GL custom layer
business_context: A B2B urban-planning SaaS overlays proposed glass towers as semi-transparent 3D meshes on a Mapbox map. Because transparent meshes are drawn in arbitrary order, overlapping towers show wrong blending: a far tower appears in front of a near one, confusing planners reviewing sightlines. City clients flagged it as a credibility issue in a $600k contract review.
constraints: Correct order-independent transparency for up to 200 overlapping translucent meshes
Implemented as a Mapbox-GL custom layer (shares the map's WebGL context)
Must not corrupt Mapbox's own depth/stencil state
60 FPS on a 4k display
Opaque terrain and roads must still occlude towers correctly

before_state.description: Transparent towers are drawn in insertion order with simple alpha blending and depth write off.

issues: Draw order is insertion order, so overlapping towers blend incorrectly
depthMask(false) globally; risks leaving Mapbox depth state dirty
No sorting and no OIT, so result depends on data order not geometry
No occlusion against opaque map features when depth write is off

reasoning: Naive approach: sort towers back-to-front on the CPU each frame by distance to camera. Rejected — per-mesh sorting is correct only for non-intersecting convex meshes; intersecting or interleaved towers still blend wrong, and it costs a sort over 200 objects every frame. Alternative: depth peeling. Rejected for cost — N passes for N layers at 4k blows the budget. Chosen path: weighted-blended OIT (McGuire/Bavoil), a single extra pass that accumulates color*weight and reveal into two render targets and composites, with state saved/restored around Mapbox. Cross-layer trace: Mapbox calls render() sharing its GL context -> we save current FBO/blend/depth -> bind an accumulation FBO with two attachments, additive blending, depth test on but write off -> draw all towers in any order writing weighted color and alpha -> a fullscreen composite blends the result over the map -> restore Mapbox's exact GL state -> opaque features still occlude via the read-only depth test -> tests assert order independence by drawing the same scene in shuffled order and comparing pixels.

after_state.description: Weighted-blended OIT: one accumulation pass plus a composite, with full Mapbox GL state save/restore.

files.purpose: Replaces order-dependent blending with an OIT accumulation+composite and brackets it with GL state save/restore.
Sets up the OIT MRTs and per-attachment blend equations for weighted accumulation.
Captures and restores Mapbox's GL state around our pass so the base map keeps rendering correctly.

migration_strategy: Add OIT layer behind flag oit_towers=off; default keeps alpha layer
Validate on a fixed scene that shuffled draw order yields identical pixels
Canary with one city client's dataset on a 4k display; check 60 FPS
Enable globally once GL-state restore verified across Mapbox versions
Remove the old alpha-blend path after a release
testing_strategy: Fixture: three overlapping translucent boxes at known depths with distinct colors. Assert: rendering them in 6 different insertion orders produces the same composited pixels (order independence). Side-effect: after render(), GL state (FBO, blend, depth mask) equals the pre-render snapshot (Mapbox state intact). Chaos test: resize the canvas mid-session and assert the OIT targets are recreated at the new size without leaking the old textures.
what_breaks_naive_approach: CPU back-to-front sorting only works for non-intersecting convex meshes; interleaved towers and intersecting geometry still blend in the wrong order, so the far tower keeps appearing in front during sightline review.

title: Dispose Three.js geometries, materials, and textures on route change to stop a 3D gallery memory leak
business_context: A real-estate platform shows 3D virtual tours; each listing mounts a Three.js scene. Navigating between listings (a common 15-30 per session) never frees GPU resources, so after ~20 listings the tab consumes 2.3GB and Chrome kills it. Bounce rate on the tour feature is 18% and correlates with session length, pointing straight at the leak.
constraints: Free all GPU resources (geometries, materials, textures, render targets) on unmount
Must not dispose shared/cached assets still used by other scenes
Works in a Next.js client component with React 18 strict mode (double-invoke effects)
Disposal must complete before the next scene allocates to avoid a memory spike
No visual fl__icker on navigation

before_state.description: Scenes are created on mount but never disposed; renderer and resources linger after unmount.

issues: No cleanup function — renderer, scene, RAF loop all leak on unmount
Geometries/materials/textures never .dispose()'d, so GPU memory grows per listing
requestAnimationFrame keeps running after unmount, retaining the whole scene
Strict mode double-invokes the effect, creating two renderers

reasoning: Naive approach: call scene.clear() on unmount. Rejected — clear() detaches objects but does not free GPU memory; geometries/materials/textures must each be .dispose()'d or the VRAM stays allocated. Alternative: reuse a single global renderer/scene and just swap models. Rejected because tours have per-listing post-processing and lights, and a shared scene leaks state between listings; also it complicates the shared-asset cache. Chosen path: a disposer that traverses the scene, disposes geometries/materials/textures (skipping ref-counted shared assets), cancels the RAF, and disposes the renderer, all in the effect cleanup, guarded for strict-mode double-invoke. Cross-layer trace: route change unmounts component -> cleanup cancels the RAF and traverses the scene disposing owned resources -> a ref-count map prevents disposing assets shared with other scenes -> renderer.dispose() and forceContextLoss() free the GL context -> strict-mode guard ensures only one renderer exists -> next scene allocates after disposal completes -> tests assert disposal counts and zero growth.

after_state.description: Effect cleanup cancels the loop and disposes all owned GPU resources with ref-counting for shared assets.

files.purpose: Adds a cleanup that cancels the loop, disposes owned resources, and tears down the renderer/context.
Traverses and disposes geometries/materials/textures while skipping anything the shared-asset cache still holds.
Tracks which assets are still referenced so disposal never frees something another scene needs.

migration_strategy: Add disposal cleanup behind flag tour_dispose=off but default on in dev to validate
Verify in strict mode that double-mount creates/disposes one renderer net
Canary on 5% of sessions; track tab memory across 30 navigations
Enable globally; keep flag a release for emergency rollback
Remove flag after memory telemetry shows flat usage across sessions
testing_strategy: Fixture: a scene with 1 shared skybox (ref-counted) and 3 owned meshes with textures. Assert: disposeScene disposes the 3 owned geometries/materials/textures and the skybox texture is NOT disposed (still shared). Side-effect: renderer.info.memory.geometries and .textures drop to the shared-only count after disposal. Chaos test: mount/unmount 50 times in a loop and assert WebGLRenderer instances and GPU geometry count return to baseline (no leak under strict-mode double-invoke).
what_breaks_naive_approach: scene.clear() only detaches objects from the graph; the underlying geometry/material/texture GPU allocations persist, so VRAM keeps climbing per listing until Chrome kills the 2.3GB tab.

title: Render 80k social-graph nodes with a WebGPU compute force-directed layout instead of CPU d3-force
business_context: A social analytics tool visualizes follower graphs as 3D node-link diagrams. d3-force on the CPU caps out around 8k nodes at interactive rates; enterprise customers have 80k-node graphs and the layout takes 40s to settle while pinning the main thread. Three enterprise renewals ($90k each) are blocked on 'the graph view is unusable at our scale'.
constraints: 80k nodes, 300k edges, layout converges in < 5s
Force simulation runs on GPU compute, not the main thread
Positions feed directly into the render buffer (no CPU readback per frame)
WebGPU required; show a clear fallback message on unsupported browsers
Deterministic given a seed so screenshots reproduce

before_state.description: d3-force runs the simulation tick on the main thread and copies positions to the GPU each frame.

issues: forceManyBody is O(n log n) per tick on the CPU — 40s to settle at 80k
Each tick copies 240k floats to the GPU, stalling the pipeline
Main thread is blocked, freezing pan/zoom during layout
No GPU parallelism for the inherently data-parallel force computation

reasoning: Naive approach: move d3-force into a Web Worker. Rejected — it unblocks the UI but the algorithm is still serial CPU and 40s; plus we still copy positions out every tick. Alternative: a Barnes-Hut quadtree on the GPU. Rejected as a v1 due to complexity; an O(n*sample) repulsion with a spatial grid converges acceptably for our graphs at far less risk. Chosen path: a WebGPU compute pipeline storing positions/velocities in storage buffers, computing repulsion (grid-bucketed) and spring forces in parallel, integrating in-place; the same buffer is bound as a vertex buffer for rendering so there is no readback. Cross-layer trace: graph loads into position/edge storage buffers -> a compute pass per frame buckets nodes into a uniform grid and accumulates repulsion + edge springs -> an integrate pass updates positions in the storage buffer -> the render pass binds that exact buffer as vertices (zero copy) -> determinism via a seeded init buffer -> unsupported browsers get a fallback notice -> tests compare GPU positions to a CPU reference for small graphs.

after_state.description: WebGPU compute force layout runs in storage buffers shared with the render pass; no per-frame CPU work.

files.purpose: Holds position/velocity/edge storage buffers and dispatches the compute layout; positions double as the vertex buffer.
Compute shader accumulating sampled repulsion and integrating positions in place with damping.
Detects WebGPU support and renders a clear fallback message when unavailable.

migration_strategy: Ship GPU layout behind flag gpu_graph=off for WebGPU browsers only
Validate convergence on a fixed 5k seed graph against the d3 reference within tolerance
Canary on enterprise tenants with >20k nodes; measure settle time
Enable for all WebGPU users; keep d3-force fallback for others
Remove main-thread d3 path for large graphs once settle time < 5s confirmed
testing_strategy: Fixture: a deterministic 200-node graph with known edges and seed. Assert: after 100 GPU steps, node positions converge to clusters matching a CPU d3 reference within a topological-similarity threshold (edge-crossing count comparable). Side-effect: no buffer is mapped/read back during stepping (zero CPU readback). Chaos test: load a graph with 50 isolated nodes (no edges) and assert they spread without NaNs and the compute pass handles count not divisible by 64.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Moving d3-force to a worker hides the freeze but the algorithm is still serial CPU at 40s for 80k nodes and still copies 240k floats out per tick, so enterprise-scale graphs never settle interactively.

title: Fix z-fighting on coplanar 3D parcel boundaries in a GIS land-registry viewer with polygon offset
business_context: A government land-registry 3D viewer draws parcel polygons and their boundary outlines on a terrain surface. Because outlines sit exactly on the parcel faces, they z-fight and flicker as the camera moves, making boundaries hard to trace. Surveyors filed 60+ tickets that boundaries 'shimmer and disappear', undermining trust in official measurements.
constraints: No flicker between coplanar fills and outlines at any zoom
Must keep precise geometry (cannot offset boundary positions, legal accuracy)
Works across the viewer's logarithmic depth buffer
Up to 50k parcels visible
Outlines must remain crisp (1-2px) and not z-bleed through terrain

before_state.description: Fills and outlines share the same depth; nothing disambiguates which wins, causing z-fighting.

issues: Fill and outline are coplanar with identical depth -> z-fighting flicker
Moving the line geometry would violate legal positional accuracy
Log depth buffer makes naive depth-bias constants behave non-linearly
No polygonOffset configured, so the GPU cannot break the tie deterministically

reasoning: Naive approach: nudge the outline geometry up by a tiny epsilon in Z. Rejected — it changes recorded boundary positions, which is legally unacceptable, and the epsilon that works at one zoom z-fights at another under the log depth buffer. Alternative: render outlines with depthTest off so they always draw on top. Rejected because then outlines bleed through terrain and parcels behind hills, which is wrong. Chosen path: keep geometry exact and use GL polygon offset to bias the fill's depth slightly away from the camera, plus draw outlines after fills with a matched depth function, all tuned for the logarithmic depth buffer. Cross-layer trace: parcel build assigns the fill material polygonOffset with factor/units pushing fills back -> outlines render in a later pass with normal depth test so they win the tie without moving -> the log-depth shader's offset is applied in clip space so it is zoom-stable -> 50k parcels still batch since only material flags change -> tests assert no depth equality between fill and outline samples.

after_state.description: Polygon offset biases fill depth so coplanar outlines win deterministically without moving geometry.

files.purpose: Applies polygon offset to fills and orders outlines after them so coplanar boundaries render crisply without moving geometry.
Documents and centralizes the log-depth configuration so the polygon-offset bias stays consistent across zoom.
Guarantees fills draw before outlines so the depth tie resolves to the outline every frame.

migration_strategy: Enable polygonOffset on fills behind flag parcel_offset=off
Verify no boundary position changed (geometry hash identical to before)
Canary on one county dataset; collect flicker reports over a week
Roll out statewide once shimmer tickets stop
Keep flag one release; remove after confirmation across zoom levels
testing_strategy: Fixture: two coplanar quads (fill + outline) at a known depth. Assert: rendered depth of outline pixels is strictly nearer than fill pixels (polygon offset pushed fills back) and boundary vertex positions are byte-identical to the input geometry. Side-effect: renderOrder sort produces fills-before-lines order. Chaos test: animate the camera from far to extreme close zoom over 200 frames and assert no frame shows fill color where an outline pixel should be (no flicker), validating zoom stability under log depth.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Nudging outline geometry in Z either corrupts legally recorded boundary positions or picks an epsilon that only avoids z-fighting at one zoom level, so the shimmer returns at other zooms under the logarithmic depth buffer.

title: Stream a 50k-row analytics scatter plot to the GPU via instanced quads instead of SVG circles
business_context: A B2B SaaS analytics dashboard plots up to 50k data points as a scatter chart. The current D3 + SVG implementation creates 50k <circle> DOM nodes, taking 6s to mount and making pan/zoom unusably laggy (sub-5 FPS). Power users with large funnels abandon the chart and export to CSV instead, defeating the product's value prop. We have 1,200 paying teams hitting this ceiling.
constraints: Render up to 50k points at 60 FPS with smooth zoom/pan
Per-point color by category and size by metric
Hover tooltip must resolve the underlying row in < 50ms
Must coexist in a React dashboard without taking over the page
Crisp anti-aliased circles, not blocky squares

before_state.description: D3 binds data to 50k SVG circles; the DOM is the bottleneck.

issues: 50k <circle> DOM nodes -> 6s mount and layout thrash
Pan/zoom re-applies attrs to 50k nodes per interaction -> < 5 FPS
Hover relies on DOM events over 50k nodes, slow and memory-heavy
No GPU acceleration; the browser composites 50k vector shapes

reasoning: Naive approach: canvas 2D with a per-point arc() draw. Rejected — 50k arc()+fill calls per frame on a 2D context is ~30ms and still misses 60 FPS during continuous zoom. Alternative: a heatmap/density binning instead of points. Rejected because users need to hover individual rows, which binning loses. Chosen path: an instanced-quad WebGL layer where each point is one instance with per-instance position/size/color, circles drawn via a fragment shader distance test for crisp AA, and hover resolved by a GPU-coordinate inverse-projection + spatial hash. Cross-layer trace: rows uploaded once into instance attributes -> zoom/pan only updates a view-transform uniform, never the buffers -> the fragment shader discards outside the radius for AA circles -> hover maps cursor to data space, queries a spatial hash for the nearest point id in O(1) -> React only hosts the canvas -> tests assert FPS, hover resolution, and that zoom touches no buffers.

after_state.description: Instanced-quad WebGL scatter: data uploaded once, zoom/pan via a uniform, hover via spatial hash.

files.purpose: Hosts the WebGL scatter, wires zoom/pan to a view transform, and resolves hover via a spatial hash.
Uploads point data once as instance attributes and redraws on zoom by updating only a view-transform uniform.
Draws each instanced quad as a smooth anti-aliased circle via a distance test with fwidth-based AA.
Provides O(1) nearest-point lookup for hover by bucketing rows into a grid.

migration_strategy: Add WebGL scatter behind flag gl_scatter=off; SVG remains default under 5k points
Canary on dashboards with >10k points; compare mount time and FPS
Verify hover resolves the same row as the SVG version on a fixed dataset
Enable for all sizes; keep SVG fallback for browsers without WebGL2
Remove SVG path for large datasets after FPS telemetry confirms 60 FPS
testing_strategy: Fixture: 50k points with known categories/positions. Assert: hover at a known data coordinate returns the correct row id from the spatial hash; zoom by 4x updates only the view uniform (spy confirms instance buffer untouched). Side-effect: one drawArraysInstanced call per frame regardless of point count. Chaos test: feed 50k points with 5% NaN metrics and assert they are skipped at upload and the chart renders the rest at 60 FPS.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Canvas-2D arc() per point is 50k fill calls per frame (~30ms), so continuous zoom still misses 60 FPS; only instanced GPU draws move the per-point cost off the per-frame CPU path.

title: Add deterministic GPU picking via an ID-render pass to a 3D BIM model with 400k components
business_context: A construction BIM viewer lets engineers click any of 400k building components (pipes, beams, ducts) to inspect metadata. Picking uses Three.js raycasting against the full scene, taking 600-1200ms per click and sometimes missing thin geometry like cables. Engineers click repeatedly, thinking the app is frozen; field usage on tablets is especially painful.
constraints: Click-to-select resolves in < 16ms regardless of component count
Pixel-accurate selection including thin/overlapping geometry
Works with instanced and merged geometry (no per-object meshes)
Tablet GPUs (limited fill rate) must stay at 30 FPS
Selection must map back to the stable BIM component GUID

before_state.description: Raycasting tests every triangle in the scene per click.

issues: intersectObjects traverses and tests millions of triangles per click -> 600-1200ms
Thin geometry (cables) is missed because the ray can pass between triangles
Merged/instanced geometry has no per-object mesh to return a GUID
Cost grows with model size; unusable at 400k components

reasoning: Naive approach: a BVH acceleration structure for raycasting (three-mesh-bvh). Rejected — it speeds raycasting greatly but still misses sub-pixel thin geometry and needs per-mesh BVHs that merged/instanced geometry lacks. Alternative: CPU-side spatial hash of bounding boxes. Rejected for the same thin-geometry miss and because box hits are not pixel-accurate. Chosen path: GPU picking — render the scene to an offscreen target with each component's GUID encoded as a unique RGBA color, then read the single pixel under the cursor and map color->GUID. Cross-layer trace: click position -> render an ID pass (same geometry, ID-color material) into a 1x1 scissor region at the cursor for cost control -> readPixels one pixel -> decode RGBA to an integer index -> map index to component GUID via a table built at load -> instanced/merged geometry encodes per-instance/per-vertex IDs so it works there too -> tablet cost bounded by the 1x1 scissor -> tests assert exact GUID for known pixels including a 1px cable.

after_state.description: GPU ID-pass picking: render GUIDs as colors, read one pixel under the cursor, decode to GUID.

files.purpose: Renders only the cursor pixel of an ID-colored scene and decodes the color back to a component GUID in constant time.
Provides a material that paints each component its unique ID color so the read-back pixel identifies it exactly.
Constructs the ID scene once with per-component ID attributes and the index->GUID table used for decoding.

migration_strategy: Build the ID scene at load behind flag gpu_pick=off; raycast remains default
Canary on desktop; verify GPU pick GUID matches raycast on 1000 known clicks
Enable on tablets and measure pick latency and 30 FPS hold
Make GPU pick the default; keep raycast fallback for WebGL1 devices
Remove raycast picking after a release of matching results
testing_strategy: Fixture: a scene with three components including a 1px-wide cable at known screen coords. Assert: pick() returns the exact GUID for each, including the cable that raycast misses. Side-effect: only a 1x1 region is rasterized (assert render target size and that readRenderTargetPixels reads 1 pixel). Chaos test: click on empty space (background) and assert the decoded ID is the reserved 0xffffff sentinel returning null, not a stale GUID.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Raycasting cost scales with triangle count and can pass between the triangles of thin cables, so at 400k components it is both slow (600ms+) and unreliable, missing exactly the thin geometry engineers need to click.

title: Replace blocking GLTF loads with a KTX2/Draco streaming pipeline for a 3D e-commerce catalog
business_context: An e-commerce site shows 3D product models on product pages. Models are raw GLTF with PNG textures averaging 28MB; on mobile the page is interactive only after a 9s blocking download and a main-thread decode that janks scrolling. Mobile conversion on 3D-enabled PDPs is 40% lower than 2D pages, and the 3D feature is on the chopping block.
constraints: Time-to-interactive under 2.5s on a mid-tier phone over 4G
Texture memory under 60MB per product on GPU
Decode off the main thread (no scroll jank)
Progressive: show a low-res model first, refine to full
Backward-compat: existing GLTF URLs continue to work during migration

before_state.description: Full-resolution GLTF + PNG loaded and decoded synchronously on the main thread.

issues: 28MB GLTF+PNG download blocks interactivity ~9s on 4G
PNG texture decode runs on the main thread, janking scroll
No compression (Draco/Meshopt) so geometry is huge
No progressive/low-res first paint

reasoning: Naive approach: lazy-load the model when the 3D viewer scrolls into view. Rejected — it defers the jank but the same 9s/28MB blocking decode still happens the moment the user reaches the viewer. Alternative: serve a single decimated low-poly model only. Rejected because shoppers zoom to inspect material detail and need full resolution on demand. Chosen path: re-encode assets to Draco/Meshopt geometry + KTX2 (Basis) GPU-compressed textures that decode on the GPU, load a low-res LOD first then stream the high-res, with KTX2 transcoding in a worker. Cross-layer trace: build step produces .glb with Meshopt geometry and KTX2 textures plus a low-res LOD -> the loader fetches the low-res LOD first for instant paint -> KTX2 textures transcode in a worker to the device's native GPU format (no main-thread PNG decode) -> the high-res LOD streams in and swaps -> old GLTF URLs still resolve via a fallback loader -> tests assert TTI, GPU format, and worker decode.

after_state.description: Meshopt + KTX2 progressive pipeline: low-res first paint, worker transcode, GPU-native textures.

files.purpose: Configures KTX2 + Meshopt decoding and loads a low-res LOD first, then the high-res, both decoded off the main thread.
Produces compressed high-res and decimated low-res GLBs with KTX2 textures at build time.
Drives progressive loading: adds the low-res model immediately and swaps in the high-res when it arrives.
Keeps un-migrated raw GLTF products working during the asset re-encoding rollout.

migration_strategy: Add KTX2/Meshopt loader behind flag asset_v2=off; products without .glb fall back to legacy GLTF
Re-encode top-100 products; canary their PDPs and measure TTI on real devices
Verify GPU texture format is the device-native compressed format (not RGBA8)
Ramp encoding to the full catalog; enable flag globally
Retire raw-PNG GLTF assets after the catalog is fully re-encoded
testing_strategy: Fixture: a product with a low (.low.glb) and high (.high.glb) LOD and a known triangle/texture count. Assert: low LOD adds to the scene first, then high LOD replaces it (assert scene.remove of low happened). Side-effect: the texture's internal format is a compressed GPU format and no PNG decode ran on the main thread (no long task). Chaos test: make the high-res fetch time out and assert the low-res stays rendered (graceful degradation) rather than blanking.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Lazy-loading only postpones the 28MB download and main-thread PNG decode to the moment the viewer scrolls in, so the same 9s block and scroll jank still hit on a mid-tier phone.

title: Implement temporal-coherent LOD selection to stop popping in a 3D game's open-world foliage
business_context: A browser-based open-world game renders dense foliage with discrete LOD levels. As the camera moves, meshes snap between LODs causing visible 'popping' that QA flagged as a launch blocker; it is most jarring on grass and shrubs near the player. The studio's player-retention model shows visual polish issues drive a 7% D1 drop.
constraints: No visible LOD pop within 40m of the camera
Maintain 60 FPS with 30k foliage instances
Deterministic given camera path (for replay/QA)
Memory must not double (cannot keep all LODs resident per instance)
Works with the existing instanced foliage renderer

before_state.description: Hard LOD switch at fixed distance thresholds with no blending, causing pops.

issues: Hard threshold causes a 1-frame geometry swap (pop) at each boundary
No hysteresis, so instances at the boundary flicker between LODs as the camera jitters
No cross-fade or dithered transition
All LODs would need to be resident to blend, doubling memory

reasoning: Naive approach: add hysteresis bands so an instance only switches LOD after passing the threshold by a margin. Rejected alone — it removes flicker at the boundary but the single-frame geometry swap (the pop) still happens, just later. Alternative: keep both LODs resident and alpha cross-fade. Rejected because it doubles geometry memory and draw cost, breaking the memory and FPS budgets. Chosen path: screen-space dithered (stochastic) LOD transition — during a transition window, render a per-pixel dither mask that fades one LOD out and the next in over several frames using a stable per-instance hash, plus hysteresis to prevent flicker; only one LOD is resident per instance at a time except during the short transition. Cross-layer trace: camera distance feeds a transition factor per instance -> a hysteresis band prevents thrash -> during the window the shader uses a screen-space dither threshold derived from a per-instance hash to discard pixels, blending LODs without true transparency -> the instance buffer carries the transition factor, not duplicate geometry -> determinism via the hash seeded on instance id -> tests assert smooth fraction and no full-frame swap.

after_state.description: Stochastic dithered LOD transition with hysteresis: smooth, deterministic, single-LOD memory.

files.purpose: Drives a timed transition factor per instance with hysteresis so LOD changes are smooth and flicker-free.
Implements screen-space dithered cross-fade so two LODs blend without true transparency or extra memory.
Stores the transition factor and per-instance hash in the instance buffer, keeping a single LOD resident.

migration_strategy: Add dithered LOD behind flag lod_dither=off; hard-switch remains default
Canary in a QA replay along a fixed camera path; visually diff for pops
Tune transition window and hysteresis bands against frame budget
Enable in a beta build; measure FPS with 30k instances
Ship to all; remove hard-switch path after QA sign-off
testing_strategy: Fixture: a deterministic camera path crossing all LOD boundaries. Assert: the per-instance transition factor ramps 0->1 over ~250ms and lod only commits when t reaches 1 (no single-frame swap). Side-effect: the instance buffer never grows (single LOD resident; only transition/hash written). Chaos test: jitter the camera back and forth across a boundary and assert hysteresis prevents more than one transition start per second (no flicker), keeping the path deterministic for replay.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hysteresis alone still performs a one-frame geometry swap when the LOD finally changes, so the visible pop remains; only a temporal dithered transition removes the snap without doubling memory.

title: Throttle and coalesce resize/devicePixelRatio changes to stop GPU thrash in a multi-pane 3D editor
business_context: A CAD-adjacent 3D editor has 4 resizable viewports. Dragging a splitter fires hundreds of resize events; each immediately reallocates render targets and resizes the WebGL canvas, causing GPU stalls, dropped frames, and occasional context loss on integrated GPUs. Designers report the UI 'chokes' while arranging panels, a top complaint in NPS detractor comments.
constraints: Resize must reallocate GPU targets at most once per animation frame
Support DPR changes (moving window between 1x and 2x monitors)
No context loss from rapid reallocations on integrated GPUs
Final size must always be exact (no off-by-one after coalescing)
Four independent viewports, each throttled separately

before_state.description: Every resize event synchronously reallocates render targets and resizes the canvas.

issues: ResizeObserver fires per pixel during a drag -> hundreds of GPU reallocations/sec
Each setSize destroys/creates GPU buffers, stalling the pipeline
Rapid reallocations can trigger context loss on integrated GPUs
No DPR handling; moving to a 2x monitor blurs or over-allocates

reasoning: Naive approach: debounce onResize by 100ms. Rejected — a flat debounce makes the viewport visibly lag the splitter during a drag and still snaps at the end; it also does not coalesce DPR changes. Alternative: resize only on pointerup. Rejected because the user sees stale content while dragging and the editor feels broken. Chosen path: coalesce all resize and DPR signals into a single pending state and apply exactly one reallocation per animation frame via requestAnimationFrame, computing the true target size at apply time so the final size is always exact. Cross-layer trace: ResizeObserver and matchMedia(DPR) updates only set a dirty flag and the latest desired size -> a per-viewport rAF scheduler applies one setSize per frame using the freshest dimensions -> integrated GPUs see at most 60 reallocations/sec instead of hundreds -> DPR changes recompute the backing-store size once -> tests assert one reallocation per frame and exact final size.

after_state.description: Resize/DPR signals coalesce into one reallocation per frame with exact final dimensions.

files.purpose: Coalesces resize/DPR events to one reallocation per frame using the freshest dimensions, with exact final size.
Detects devicePixelRatio changes when the window moves between monitors and signals a single reallocation.
Bounds total render-target reallocations per frame across all four viewports as a safety valve.

migration_strategy: Add coalesced resize behind flag resize_coalesce=off per viewport
Canary in the editor; record GPU reallocation count during a splitter drag
Verify final sizes are pixel-exact after a drag (no off-by-one)
Enable globally; watch context-loss telemetry on integrated GPUs
Remove synchronous resize after a release with zero resize-induced context losses
testing_strategy: Fixture: a fake ResizeObserver that emits 200 size events in one frame ending at 813x517. Assert: setSize/rt.setSize is called exactly once and the final size is 813x517 (exact). Side-effect: render-target reallocation count equals 1 per frame regardless of event count. Chaos test: emit resize events continuously across 10 frames plus a DPR change to 2x mid-stream and assert at most one reallocation per frame and the backing store ends at the correct 2x dimensions.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A flat 100ms debounce makes the viewport visibly lag the splitter and still does not bound reallocations per frame, while resize-on-pointerup shows stale content during the drag; only per-frame coalescing both bounds GPU work and stays responsive.

title: Add frustum + occlusion culling with GPU queries to a 3D warehouse digital twin
business_context: A logistics digital twin renders a 200k-square-meter warehouse with 60k rack/shelf meshes. The renderer draws everything every frame even though 80% is behind walls or off-screen, holding only 24 FPS on the operations wall display. Operators need smooth real-time AGV tracking overlaid on the twin; the judder makes collision alerts hard to read.
constraints: Cull off-screen and fully occluded geometry, target 60 FPS
Occlusion result must not lag more than 1 frame (no visible pop-in of visible racks)
Works with instanced racks (cannot cull per-instance on CPU at this scale)
Reversible: any culling bug must be flippable off without redeploy
AGV overlay (small, always visible) must never be culled

before_state.description: All meshes drawn every frame; no frustum or occlusion culling.

issues: No occlusion culling: 80% of racks behind walls are fully shaded
Per-object frustum culling still iterates 60k objects on the CPU each frame
Instanced racks can't be frustum-culled per-instance by three.js
GPU fill-rate wasted on hidden geometry -> 24 FPS

reasoning: Naive approach: rely on three.js per-object frustumCulled. Rejected — it skips off-screen objects but does nothing for on-screen-but-occluded racks behind walls, which is the bulk of the waste, and it still loops 60k objects. Alternative: precomputed PVS (potentially visible sets) per zone. Rejected because the warehouse layout changes (racks move), so a static PVS goes stale and needs rebaking. Chosen path: hierarchical culling — CPU frustum-cull cluster bounding volumes, then GPU occlusion queries against a depth pre-pass for the surviving clusters, using last-frame results to avoid stalls. Cross-layer trace: build a cluster BVH over racks -> each frame, frustum-cull cluster bounds on the CPU (cheap, 60k -> hundreds of clusters) -> render a coarse depth pre-pass of occluders (walls) -> issue GPU occlusion queries per visible cluster, reading last frame's results to avoid GPU sync stalls -> only clusters with passed samples are drawn -> the AGV overlay is in an always-visible layer -> a flag disables culling instantly -> tests assert hidden clusters are skipped and visible ones are never dropped.

after_state.description: Hierarchical frustum + GPU occlusion-query culling on rack clusters, using last-frame results to avoid stalls.

files.purpose: Frustum-culls clusters on the CPU and uses last-frame GPU occlusion-query results to skip occluded clusters without stalling.
Builds a depth buffer of large occluders so occlusion queries can determine which clusters are hidden.
Orchestrates the cull/draw order and guarantees the AGV overlay is always rendered.

migration_strategy: Add cluster BVH + culler behind flag occlusion_cull=off; default draws all
Canary on the ops wall display; verify no visible rack pop-in/out
Tune cluster granularity vs query overhead; measure FPS
Enable globally with the runtime kill-switch wired to the flag
Remove the draw-everything path after a week of stable 60 FPS
testing_strategy: Fixture: a scene with a wall occluding 100 rack clusters and 20 visible clusters. Assert: after two frames (so queries resolve) the 100 hidden clusters are excluded from draw and all 20 visible ones are drawn. Side-effect: the AGV overlay layer is always in the draw list regardless of culling. Chaos test: flip culler.enabled=false mid-run and assert all clusters draw again immediately (kill-switch works) with no query handles leaked.
what_breaks_naive_approach: three.js per-object frustum culling does nothing for on-screen-but-occluded racks behind walls — the dominant waste here — and still iterates all 60k objects, so the fill-rate bottleneck and 24 FPS remain.

title: Render server-side 3D thumbnails for an asset marketplace with headless WebGL in a worker queue
business_context: A 3D asset marketplace needs preview thumbnails for 2M user-uploaded models. Today thumbnails are captured client-side when a model first loads, so models that never get viewed have no preview, and search results show gray placeholders. Search CTR is 30% lower on placeholder cards. The team needs reliable server-rendered thumbnails generated on upload.
constraints: Render a 512x512 thumbnail per uploaded model within 8s of upload
Handle GLTF/GLB/OBJ/FBX with consistent camera framing
Run headless on Linux workers (no display) with GPU or software fallback
Throughput: 20k thumbnails/day, autoscaling workers
Deterministic framing so re-renders match (cache keys stable)

before_state.description: Thumbnails are captured in the browser only when a user opens the model.

issues: Only runs when a user opens the model in a browser -> most assets have no thumbnail
Camera framing depends on whatever view the user had -> inconsistent previews
Cannot run server-side; depends on a DOM canvas and a visible tab
No queue/retry; a failed capture leaves a permanent placeholder

reasoning: Naive approach: spin up a headless Chrome per model to reuse the client capture path. Rejected — a full browser per render is ~300MB and 3-5s of startup overhead, far too heavy for 20k/day and fragile under load. Alternative: a native renderer (Blender CLI). Rejected because it cannot reuse our Three.js material/lighting setup, so server thumbnails would not match the in-app look. Chosen path: headless Three.js with the `headless-gl`/EGL WebGL context in a worker queue, a deterministic auto-framing camera (fit to bounding sphere), and a software fallback when no GPU is present. Cross-layer trace: upload event enqueues a render job -> a worker loads the model with the shared GLTF/OBJ loaders into a headless GL context -> a deterministic camera frames the bounding sphere at a fixed azimuth/elevation -> the framebuffer is read back and encoded to PNG/WebP -> stored at a content-addressed key so re-renders are cache hits -> failures retry with backoff and a software-GL fallback -> tests assert framing determinism and pixel readback.

after_state.description: Worker-queue headless renderer with deterministic framing, software fallback, and content-addressed storage.

files.purpose: Renders a 512x512 thumbnail in a headless GL context and reads back the framebuffer to a PNG buffer.
Computes a reproducible camera that frames any model identically, making thumbnails consistent and cacheable.
Drives the queue with idempotent cache-keyed storage and retry/backoff so failures do not leave placeholders.
Enqueues a thumbnail job at upload time so every asset gets a preview without a user view.

migration_strategy: Deploy thumbnail workers consuming a new queue; backfill is paused
Enqueue thumbnails for new uploads only; verify framing on a sample set
Backfill historical assets in batches at low priority; monitor worker autoscaling
Switch search cards to server thumbnails behind flag server_thumbs=off, ramping
Keep client capture as a fallback for unsupported formats; remove placeholders after backfill
testing_strategy: Fixture: a GLB cube model with known bounds. Assert: frameModel produces a camera whose projected cube fills ~80% of the 512x512 frame, and rendering twice yields byte-identical PNGs (determinism). Side-effect: consume() writes exactly one object to storage and is a no-op on a second call (idempotent cache key). Chaos test: feed a corrupt FBX and assert the consumer retries 3x then marks failed without crashing the worker or leaking the GL context.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A headless Chrome per model costs ~300MB and seconds of startup, so at 20k/day it cannot scale and falls over under load; a lightweight headless-GL worker is the only path that meets throughput.

title: Fix precision jitter on a planet-scale 3D map by adopting camera-relative floating-origin rendering
business_context: A satellite-tracking app renders objects in Earth-centered coordinates (meters from Earth's core, ~6.4M+). At those magnitudes float32 vertex positions lose precision, so models visibly jitter and vibrate when zoomed in to a satellite. Aerospace customers running orbital rendezvous reviews say the jitter makes close-approach analysis impossible.
constraints: Sub-meter visual stability when zoomed to a single satellite
Keep world data in double-precision ECEF coordinates (authoritative)
No change to the physics/propagation pipeline (separate service)
Works with three.js which uses float32 internally
Support 5k tracked objects without per-object CPU recompute each frame

before_state.description: Positions are passed to three.js as raw ECEF meters; float32 precision loss causes jitter.

issues: ECEF magnitudes ~6.4e6 m exceed float32's ~7 significant digits -> cm-level jitter
Camera also at huge coordinates compounds the error in the view matrix
three.js matrices are float32, so no amount of double input survives
Jitter is worst exactly when zoomed in, where precision matters most

reasoning: Naive approach: scale the whole world down (e.g. divide by 1000). Rejected — scaling shifts the precision problem but does not remove it; at km-from-core you still exceed float32 near satellites, and it breaks unit consistency with the physics service. Alternative: use a logarithmic depth buffer. Rejected because that fixes depth precision, not position precision; the XY jitter remains. Chosen path: floating-origin / camera-relative rendering — keep authoritative positions in double-precision JS numbers, and each frame subtract the camera's double-precision world position before converting to float32 for the GPU, so rendered coordinates are small and precise near the camera. Cross-layer trace: physics service supplies double ECEF -> a world-origin manager holds the camera's double position -> per frame, each object's render position = (objectEcef - cameraEcef) computed in doubles, then cast to float32 -> the camera sits at the origin in render space -> three.js matrices now operate on small numbers with full precision -> 5k objects updated via a typed-array transform, not per-object Object3D math -> tests assert sub-meter stability.

after_state.description: Camera-relative floating origin: authoritative doubles, per-frame double subtraction, small precise float32 render coords.

files.purpose: Holds the double-precision camera origin and converts authoritative positions into small camera-relative coordinates.
Stores authoritative double positions and writes camera-relative matrices for all objects each frame via instancing.
Keeps the GPU camera at the origin and feeds the camera's true double position into the floating origin.

migration_strategy: Add floating-origin layer behind flag float_origin=off; default keeps raw ECEF
Validate sub-meter stability when zoomed to a single satellite vs the old jitter
Canary with one aerospace customer's tracking dataset
Enable globally; keep raw-ECEF path for far/global zoom where it is fine
Remove raw-ECEF placement after stability is confirmed at all zoom levels
testing_strategy: Fixture: two objects 5m apart at ECEF ~7e6 m with the camera 50m away. Assert: their camera-relative float32 positions differ by ~5m within < 1cm error (precision preserved), whereas raw float32 ECEF loses >10cm. Side-effect: the GPU camera position is exactly (0,0,0) after sync (origin moved to data). Chaos test: jump the camera 10,000 km in one frame and assert objects re-place correctly without a precision spike (origin recomputed from doubles).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Uniformly scaling the world only relocates the precision cliff; near a satellite the coordinates still exceed float32's ~7 digits, so the jitter returns exactly where rendezvous analysis needs stability.

title: Throttle a 3D telemetry viewer to a dynamic resolution scaler to hold 60 FPS under load
business_context: A motorsport telemetry tool renders a 3D track with live car positions and a dense data overlay. On a busy race weekend the scene load varies wildly; during pile-ups the frame time spikes to 40ms and the live overlay stutters at the worst moment. Engineers on the pit wall need a steady frame rate to read closing speeds; judder costs them split-second decisions.
constraints: Hold 60 FPS by scaling render resolution dynamically, never the overlay
Resolution changes must be imperceptible (no visible blur snap)
Scale range 0.5x to 1.0x of native, in small steps
React within 3 frames to a load spike
UI/overlay text stays at native resolution always

before_state.description: Renders at fixed native resolution; frame time spikes under load with no adaptation.

issues: Fixed native resolution; no headroom when load spikes
Overlay rendered at the same scale as the 3D scene, so scaling it would blur text
No frame-time feedback loop to drive resolution
Spikes during pile-ups blow the budget with no recovery mechanism

reasoning: Naive approach: lower the pixel ratio permanently to leave headroom. Rejected — it sacrifices sharpness all the time for the rare spike, and engineers complained the track looked soft. Alternative: drop the overlay update rate under load. Rejected because the overlay (closing speeds) is the most important element and must stay crisp and current. Chosen path: a dynamic resolution scaler driven by a smoothed frame-time signal that lowers the 3D render-target scale toward 0.5x when over budget and raises it back when there is headroom, with the overlay drawn separately at native resolution. Cross-layer trace: each frame measures GPU+CPU time -> an EMA smooths it to avoid reacting to single-frame noise -> a controller nudges the scale down when over 16.6ms and up when comfortably under, in small steps to avoid visible snaps -> the 3D scene renders to a scaled offscreen target that is upsampled, while the overlay layer composites at native resolution -> tests assert the scaler converges and the overlay stays native.

after_state.description: Frame-time-driven dynamic resolution scaler; 3D target scales, overlay stays native.

files.purpose: Drives a smoothed controller that lowers/raises render scale in small steps to track the frame budget.
Renders the 3D scene into a dynamically-sized target and composites the native-resolution overlay on top.
