Keeps the data overlay crisp at native resolution independent of the 3D scene's dynamic scale.

migration_strategy: Add dynamic resolution behind flag dyn_res=off; default native fixed
Canary on the pit-wall display; record scale histogram and FPS during a session
Tune step size and EMA so scale changes are imperceptible
Enable for race weekends; verify overlay stays native sharp
Remove fixed-resolution path after a clean event
testing_strategy: Fixture: a synthetic frame-time stream that spikes to 30ms for 10 frames then recovers. Assert: the scaler drops scale toward 0.5 within 3 frames of the spike and recovers to 1.0 after, in 0.05 steps. Side-effect: the overlay canvas transform stays at native DPR regardless of scene scale. Chaos test: feed an oscillating frame time around the target and assert the scale does not oscillate visibly (EMA dampens it), staying within one step band.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Permanently lowering pixel ratio makes the track look soft all the time to handle a rare pile-up spike, and throttling the overlay degrades exactly the closing-speed data engineers depend on — neither adapts to load the way a dynamic scaler does.

title: Migrate a custom Three.js shader from WebGL GLSL to WebGPU WGSL with a runtime backend abstraction
business_context: A data-viz platform has a custom volumetric shader (medical CT volume rendering) written in GLSL for WebGLRenderer. Chrome is deprecating some WebGL paths and the team wants WebGPU for 3x throughput on large volumes, but cannot drop WebGL for Safari users. Maintaining two divergent shader codebases caused 3 visual-regression incidents last quarter where the backends rendered differently.
constraints: Single source of truth for shader logic across WebGL and WebGPU
Identical visual output on both backends (golden-image parity)
Runtime backend selection based on capability
Volume textures up to 512^3 (134M voxels)
No regression in existing WebGL Safari rendering

before_state.description: Two hand-maintained shader codebases (GLSL and an early WGSL port) drift apart.

issues: A separate WGSL port exists and has already drifted (different step accumulation)
No shared source -> visual regressions when one side is edited
Backend choice is hardcoded, not capability-based
512^3 volumes need WebGPU throughput but WebGL must remain for Safari

reasoning: Naive approach: keep both shaders and add a code-review checklist to keep them in sync. Rejected — process did not prevent the 3 regressions; humans drift. Alternative: drop WebGL entirely and require WebGPU. Rejected because Safari users (a third of medical clients) would lose the feature. Chosen path: author the ray-march in a small shared intermediate (a templated shader source with a thin GLSL/WGSL codegen for the few syntax differences) plus a backend abstraction that picks WebGPU when available and WebGL otherwise, validated by golden-image parity tests. Cross-layer trace: shader logic lives in one template with backend-neutral functions -> a codegen emits GLSL and WGSL from the same source, differing only in type/qualifier syntax -> a Backend interface (createPipeline, setUniform, draw) abstracts WebGLRenderer vs WebGPURenderer -> capability detection chooses the backend at init -> 512^3 volumes route to WebGPU storage textures -> golden-image tests render the same fixture on both and diff -> tests fail the build if outputs diverge.

after_state.description: Single templated shader source emits GLSL+WGSL; a backend abstraction selects WebGPU/WebGL with golden-image parity.

files.purpose: Holds the backend-neutral ray-march logic once so both targets are generated from identical source.
Generates GLSL and WGSL from the shared source, isolating the few backend syntax differences in one place.
Chooses WebGPU when supported and WebGL otherwise, behind a uniform backend interface.
Build-gating test that renders a fixture on both backends and fails if outputs diverge.

migration_strategy: Introduce shared shader source generating GLSL identical to today's; verify byte-equivalent WebGL output
Add WebGPU backend behind flag webgpu=off for capable browsers
Run golden-image parity tests in CI; block merges on divergence
Ramp WebGPU to capable users; Safari stays on WebGL
Delete the drifted standalone WGSL file once codegen is authoritative
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 64^3 synthetic volume with a known density gradient. Assert: WebGL and WebGPU renders match within 1% per-pixel (golden parity) and the generated GLSL produces the same image as the legacy hand-written GLSL. Side-effect: codegen output for both targets is deterministic (same hash across runs). Chaos test: edit the shared shader body (change STEPS) and assert both backends regenerate and still match each other, proving they cannot drift independently.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A review checklist relies on humans to keep two shader codebases in sync and has already failed three times; only a single generated source plus a parity gate structurally prevents backend divergence.

title: Pre-warm shader compilation to remove first-interaction hitches in a 3D onboarding tour
business_context: A SaaS product has a 3D onboarding tour. The first time a user hovers or clicks an element, a new material variant compiles its shader on the spot, causing a 200-500ms hitch right during the critical first impression. Activation analytics show a 6% drop-off precisely at the first interactive step where the hitch occurs.
constraints: Zero shader-compile hitch on first user interaction
Pre-warm must not delay initial page load by more than 400ms
Cover all material/light/shadow permutations actually used in the tour
Works with three.js's on-demand program compilation
Pre-warm runs off the critical render path

before_state.description: Shaders compile lazily the first time each material/light combination is rendered.

issues: Hover/click swap in new material variants that compile on demand -> 200-500ms hitch
Compilation happens on the main thread during the first interaction
No pre-warm of the light/shadow permutations the tour uses
Hitch lands exactly at the first activation step

reasoning: Naive approach: render the scene once off-screen at load to trigger compilation. Rejected — a single render only compiles the initial visible permutations, not the hover/selected variants that appear later, so the first-hover hitch persists. Alternative: precompile a giant uber-shader with all features enabled. Rejected because it bloats every draw with unused branches and hurts steady-state FPS. Chosen path: enumerate every material variant and light setup the tour will use and call renderer.compile() (or compileAsync) on a hidden warm-up scene during idle time after first paint, so all programs exist before any interaction. Cross-layer trace: a manifest lists all variants (base, hover-emissive, selected-outline) and the light rig -> after first paint, an idle callback builds a hidden scene with each variant under the real lights and calls renderer.compileAsync -> the WebGL programs are cached so later swaps reuse them -> the warm-up runs off the critical path and is time-boxed -> tests assert no new program compiles on first interaction.

after_state.description: All tour shader variants compiled during idle after first paint via a manifest-driven warm-up.

files.purpose: Builds a hidden scene with every material variant under the real lights and compiles all programs ahead of interaction.
Single source listing all variants so warm-up provably covers every program the tour can trigger.
Runs the warm-up off the critical path during idle so initial load is unaffected.

migration_strategy: Add warm-up behind flag shader_warmup=off; default lazy compile
Verify the manifest covers all variants via a coverage assertion in CI
Canary on new signups; measure first-interaction hitch in RUM
Enable globally once hitch p95 < 16ms on first interaction
Keep the manifest test as a guard so new variants must be registered
testing_strategy: Fixture: a tour with base + hover + selected variants and the real light rig. Assert: after warmUp resolves, renderer.info.programs contains a program for each variant. Side-effect: triggering a hover swap compiles zero new programs (program count unchanged). Chaos test: add a new material variant not in the manifest and assert the CI coverage test fails, forcing registration so no variant compiles during interaction.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single off-screen render only compiles the initially visible permutations, so the hover/selected variants still compile on first interaction — the manifest-driven warm-up is what guarantees every variant is precompiled.

title: Build a tile-based 3D heatmap aggregation pipeline for a 100M-row geospatial dataset
business_context: A logistics analytics product visualizes 100M delivery events as a 3D extruded hex heatmap. The client currently fetches raw points and aggregates in the browser, downloading 1.4GB and freezing for 18s before the first render. Operations managers comparing regions abandon the view; it is the most-requested-but-least-used feature per product analytics.
constraints: First render under 3s regardless of dataset size
Aggregation happens server-side per zoom/viewport tile
3D extrusion height encodes event count; color encodes a second metric
Pan/zoom fetches only newly visible tiles
Tile cache must invalidate when new events arrive (max 5-min staleness)

before_state.description: Client downloads all raw events and aggregates into hex bins in JS.

issues: Downloads 1.4GB of raw events before any aggregation
Aggregates 100M rows on the main thread -> 18s freeze
No viewport/zoom awareness; fetches the whole world always
Re-fetches everything on any pan/zoom

reasoning: Naive approach: move the JS aggregation into a Web Worker. Rejected — it unfreezes the UI but still downloads 1.4GB and re-aggregates 100M rows per view, missing the 3s budget. Alternative: precompute one global aggregation at a fixed resolution. Rejected because zooming in needs finer hex resolution and the global aggregate cannot provide it. Chosen path: server-side tiled aggregation (a materialized hex-bin rollup per zoom level), with the client requesting only visible tiles, a tile cache keyed by zoom/bbox/version, and incremental invalidation as events arrive. Cross-layer trace: viewport+zoom compute the set of visible tile keys -> the client requests missing tiles from an aggregation API -> the API serves pre-rolled hex bins for that tile/zoom from a materialized table (refreshed every 5 min) -> the 3D layer extrudes each hex by count and colors by metric -> pan/zoom fetches only new tiles, reusing cached ones -> a version stamp invalidates stale tiles -> tests assert correct bins and tile-only fetches.

after_state.description: Server-side tiled hex aggregation; client fetches only visible tiles with versioned caching.

files.content:
-- Materialized hex-bin rollup per zoom level, refreshed every 5 minutes.
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW hex_rollup AS
SELECT zoom, h3_index, COUNT(*) AS cnt, AVG(delivery_minutes) AS avg_min,
       tile_x, tile_y
FROM delivery_events
CROSS JOIN LATERAL h3_cells(lat, lng) AS h3_index
GROUP BY zoom, h3_index, tile_x, tile_y;
-- API queries: SELECT ... WHERE zoom=$1 AND tile_x=$2 AND tile_y=$3;
files.purpose: Pre-aggregates 100M events into hex bins per zoom and tile so the client never aggregates raw data.
Serves pre-rolled bins for a single tile with a version stamp for cache invalidation.
Fetches only newly visible tiles, caches them by key, and invalidates stale tiles by version.
Renders the tile's hex bins as an instanced extruded prism mesh with height/color encodings.

migration_strategy: Build the materialized hex rollup and tile API; keep client aggregation live
Ship tiled HexLayer behind flag tiled_heatmap=off; compare bins to client aggregation
Canary on one region; measure first-render time and pan/zoom fetch volume
Enable globally; wire the 5-min refresh and version invalidation
Remove client-side raw aggregation after parity is confirmed
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 10k-event dataset with a known hex distribution. Assert: a tile request returns bins whose counts match a direct aggregation of the events in that tile; the 3D layer extrudes the highest-count hex tallest. Side-effect: panning to an adjacent tile fetches only the new tile (cached tiles not re-requested). Chaos test: bump the rollup version mid-session and assert only stale-version tiles for that zoom are invalidated and re-fetched, not the whole cache.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Moving aggregation to a worker still downloads 1.4GB and re-aggregates 100M rows per view, blowing the 3s budget; only server-side tiled rollups bound the client work to the visible viewport.

title: Add cross-tab GPU resource coordination to stop context loss when multiple 3D dashboards open
business_context: A monitoring SaaS lets users open multiple dashboard tabs, each with a live 3D scene. Power users open 6-10 tabs; combined GPU memory exceeds the browser's per-origin budget and tabs randomly lose their WebGL context and go blank. The on-call team gets paged for 'dashboards going blank' that are actually GPU-budget exhaustion across tabs.
constraints: Prevent context loss by coordinating GPU memory budget across same-origin tabs
Background tabs must yield GPU resources to the focused tab
Re-acquire resources within 1s when a tab regains focus
Coordination survives a tab crash (no permanent leak of the budget)
No server round-trip; coordination is client-side only

before_state.description: Each tab allocates GPU resources independently with no awareness of other tabs.

issues: Each tab loads full-resolution textures regardless of how many tabs are open
Background tabs keep their full GPU footprint, summing past the origin budget
No coordination -> the browser drops a random tab's context
No re-acquisition logic when a tab is refocused

reasoning: Naive approach: just free resources on visibilitychange when a tab is hidden. Rejected — it helps but tabs do not know the global budget; many visible-but-idle tabs can still sum past the limit, and re-acquisition is uncoordinated, causing thrash. Alternative: a SharedWorker that owns all GPU contexts. Rejected because WebGL contexts cannot be shared across tabs and a SharedWorker cannot render to per-tab canvases. Chosen path: a BroadcastChannel-based budget coordinator where each tab registers its requested budget, a leader-elected coordinator allocates shares (focused tab gets priority), and tabs downgrade texture resolution when over budget; a heartbeat reclaims budget from crashed tabs. Cross-layer trace: tab open broadcasts a 'register' with desired budget -> the elected leader sums requests and replies with each tab's granted budget -> tabs at/over budget downgrade to lower-res textures or release render targets -> focus change re-broadcasts priority so the focused tab gets full budget within 1s -> heartbeats detect a dead tab and free its share -> tests assert total budget never exceeds the cap and refocus restores resolution.

after_state.description: BroadcastChannel budget coordinator allocates GPU shares across tabs with focus priority and crash recovery.

files.purpose: Coordinates a shared GPU budget across same-origin tabs, granting the focused tab priority and pruning crashed tabs via heartbeat.
Registers each tab's GPU demand and downgrades texture resolution to the granted budget, preventing origin-wide exhaustion.
Frees a tab's budget share immediately on close so remaining tabs can scale up.

migration_strategy: Add the coordinator behind flag gpu_coord=off; default independent allocation
Canary with internal power users opening 8+ tabs; measure context-loss rate
Tune cap and min-share to the observed origin budget
Enable globally; verify refocus restores high-res within 1s
Remove independent allocation after context-loss pages drop to zero
testing_strategy: Fixture: three simulated tabs sharing a 256MB cap, each wanting 192MB. Assert: the sum of granted budgets never exceeds the cap, and the focused tab is granted more than background tabs. Side-effect: when the focused tab changes, the newly focused tab's grant rises and the old one's falls within one heartbeat. Chaos test: kill a tab without a 'bye' (simulate crash) and assert its share is reclaimed after the 3s heartbeat timeout, so the budget is not permanently leaked.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Freeing only on visibilitychange ignores the global budget, so many visible-but-idle tabs still sum past the origin GPU limit and the browser drops a random context — only cross-tab coordination caps the aggregate footprint.

title: Replace per-frame line geometry rebuilds with a GPU-thickened line shader in a 3D EKG monitor
business_context: A healthcare telemetry wall shows live 3D EKG/vitals waveforms for 48 ICU beds. Each waveform rebuilds its line BufferGeometry every frame to scroll new samples, allocating and uploading 48 buffers per frame and causing a GC sawtooth that drops the wall to 22 FPS during alarms. Clinicians need rock-steady traces; jitter on a vitals wall is a patient-safety concern.
constraints: 48 simultaneous scrolling waveforms at a steady 60 FPS
Constant-width lines (2-3px) regardless of zoom, on GPU
No per-frame geometry allocation (flat memory)
New samples append without rebuilding the whole buffer
Works on the wall's WebGL2 hardware

before_state.description: Each frame rebuilds the full line geometry from the sample ring buffer and re-uploads it.

issues: Allocates a new Float32Array every frame per waveform -> GC sawtooth
Full buffer re-upload each frame for 48 waveforms
THREE.Line width is always 1px on most platforms (no thick lines)
Scrolling rebuilds the entire geometry instead of appending

reasoning: Naive approach: reuse one Float32Array and only memmove the samples. Rejected — it removes the allocation but still re-uploads the whole buffer each frame and cannot give constant-width lines, which clinicians require. Alternative: use Line2/LineMaterial (fat lines) but keep rebuilding. Rejected because Line2 still needs geometry updates and per-frame rebuilds dominate. Chosen path: a fixed-size ring buffer uploaded once, with a 'head' uniform that the vertex shader uses to map sample index to screen X (scrolling without rebuild), and a GPU line-thickening expansion (extrude each segment to a quad in the shader) for constant width. Cross-layer trace: samples written into a pre-allocated GPU buffer at a ring head -> a 'uHead' uniform advances each frame so the shader reads the ring in scroll order -> the vertex shader expands each segment into a screen-space quad of fixed pixel width -> only the newly arrived samples are written via bufferSubData -> 48 waveforms each do one tiny sub-upload -> tests assert no per-frame full upload and constant width.

after_state.description: Ring-buffer waveform with a scroll uniform and GPU line thickening; only new samples are sub-uploaded.

files.purpose: Maintains a pre-allocated ring buffer and sub-uploads only new samples, eliminating per-frame allocation and full uploads.
Maps ring indices to a scrolling X via uHead and sets up constant-pixel-width line expansion in clip space.
Coordinates 48 ring-buffer waveforms so the whole wall renders with constant-width lines and no allocations.

migration_strategy: Add ring-buffer waveform behind flag gpu_lines=off; default per-frame rebuild
Canary on one ICU bay; record GC pauses and FPS during a simulated alarm
Verify trace shape matches the old renderer on a recorded sample stream
Enable wall-wide; confirm steady 60 FPS
Remove the rebuild path after a clean week
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 4096-sample ring with a known sine stream appended 16 samples/frame. Assert: after appends the buffer content matches the expected ring layout and uHead advances correctly; the rendered line is the configured pixel width at two zoom levels. Side-effect: only bufferSubData (not bufferData) is called per frame and no Float32Array is allocated in the loop. Chaos test: append a burst larger than the remaining ring space and assert the wraparound split writes correctly without corrupting the trace.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reusing one array removes allocation but still re-uploads the entire buffer every frame for 48 waveforms and cannot produce constant-width lines, so the wall still drops frames and traces stay 1px thin.

title: Add cascaded shadow maps to fix blocky shadows in a 3D city sun-study tool
business_context: An architecture B2B tool runs solar shadow studies on 3D city blocks. A single shadow map covers the whole scene, so near buildings have pixelated, crawling shadows while far ones look fine, and architects cannot judge shadow accuracy on a proposed tower. A $250k municipal contract requires sub-meter shadow accuracy near street level.
constraints: Sub-meter shadow resolution near the camera, scene-wide coverage
60 FPS with a 3km city extent
Stable shadows under sun animation (no crawling/swimming)
Works with the existing directional sun light
Memory budget: max 4 shadow cascades at 2048x2048

before_state.description: One directional shadow map stretched over the whole 3km scene.

issues: One 2048 map over 3km = ~1.5m per texel -> blocky near-field shadows
Shadows crawl/swim as the sun animates (no texel snapping)
No focus on the camera's near region where accuracy matters
Far and near share resolution, wasting texels on distant blocks

reasoning: Naive approach: increase the single map to 8192x8192. Rejected — 8192 over 3km is still ~0.37m/texel only marginally better, costs 4x memory, and does not fix swimming. Alternative: per-building local shadow maps. Rejected as unscalable for thousands of buildings. Chosen path: cascaded shadow maps (CSM) splitting the view frustum into distance bands, each with its own tight shadow camera, plus texel snapping to stop swimming. Cross-layer trace: the view frustum is split into N depth ranges -> each cascade fits a shadow camera tightly to its slice so near cascades get sub-meter resolution -> the fragment shader selects the cascade by depth and samples its map -> shadow cameras snap to texel grid to eliminate crawling under sun motion -> 4 cascades at 2048 stay in budget -> tests assert near-field texel density and stability.

after_state.description: Cascaded shadow maps with per-slice tight cameras and texel snapping for stable, high-resolution near shadows.

files.purpose: Splits the frustum into cascades with tight per-slice shadow cameras and texel snapping for stable near shadows.
Quantizes shadow-camera motion to texel increments, eliminating shadow swimming during sun animation.
Selects the correct cascade per fragment by view depth so near pixels sample the high-resolution map.

migration_strategy: Add CSM behind flag csm=off; default single map
Validate near-field texel density (<1m) and far coverage on a reference block
Canary on the municipal dataset; check 60 FPS over the 3km extent
Enable texel snapping and verify no shadow crawl under animated sun
Remove single-map path after architects sign off on accuracy
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 3km scene with a 50m tower and a fixed sun angle. Assert: the nearest cascade gives < 1m world units per texel at the tower base, and shadow edges are crisp vs the blocky single-map baseline. Side-effect: snapToTexel quantizes the cascade camera position to texel multiples (assert position is a texel-grid value). Chaos test: animate the sun across 100 frames and assert the shadow boundary on a static wall moves smoothly without per-frame jitter (swimming eliminated).
what_breaks_naive_approach: An 8192 single map still spends most texels on distant blocks and only reaches ~0.37m/texel near the camera while costing 4x memory and not fixing swimming, so sub-meter stable near shadows remain impossible.

title: Build a deterministic replay buffer for a 3D physics sandbox to enable shareable simulations
business_context: A devtools company offers a 3D physics playground for teaching. Users want to share simulations via URL, but the physics (ammo.js) runs at the browser's variable frame rate so the same scene evolves differently on each machine, making shared links useless. Support gets weekly 'my shared sim looks different' complaints, undermining the collaboration feature they marketed.
constraints: Bit-identical simulation given the same seed and input across machines
Fixed-timestep physics decoupled from render frame rate
Replay from a compact event log (not full state dumps)
Support up to 5k rigid bodies
Deterministic across Chrome/Firefox/Safari

before_state.description: Physics steps once per render frame at variable dt, so evolution depends on frame rate.

issues: Variable dt -> different integration on different machines/frame rates
stepSimulation with maxSubSteps=1 drops or doubles physics steps
No event log, so a sim cannot be replayed or shared deterministically
Floating-point differences accumulate without a fixed timestep

reasoning: Naive approach: just lower maxSubSteps variance by clamping dt. Rejected — clamping reduces but does not remove frame-rate dependence; the accumulated steps still differ per machine. Alternative: snapshot full world state periodically and replay snapshots. Rejected because 5k bodies make snapshots large and they still drift between snapshots. Chosen path: a fixed-timestep accumulator that steps physics in deterministic 1/120s increments regardless of render rate, plus an input event log (seed + timestamped user forces) that fully reconstructs the run, with render interpolation for smoothness. Cross-layer trace: render frames feed elapsed time into an accumulator -> the accumulator drains in fixed 1/120s physics steps so the step count is a function of simulated time only -> user inputs are recorded with their fixed-step index into an event log -> a shared URL encodes seed + event log -> replay re-runs the same fixed steps applying logged inputs at the same indices -> render interpolates between the last two physics states -> tests assert identical body positions across runs.

after_state.description: Fixed-timestep accumulator + seeded input event log yields bit-identical, shareable simulations with render interpolation.

files.purpose: Drains real elapsed time into deterministic fixed physics steps so the step sequence is frame-rate independent.
Records seed and per-step inputs so a run can be reconstructed exactly from a compact shareable string.
Runs physics on fixed steps applying logged inputs by step index, with interpolation alpha for smooth rendering.

migration_strategy: Introduce fixed-step loop behind flag deterministic_sim=off
Verify a recorded run replays bit-identically on Chrome/Firefox/Safari
Enable shareable URL encoding the seed + event log
Ramp to all users; keep variable-dt loop for non-shared scratch sims
Remove variable-dt loop once shared-sim complaints stop
testing_strategy: Fixture: a seeded scene with 100 bodies and a logged impulse at step 50. Assert: running the loop twice (different simulated frame rates) yields identical body positions at step 200 (bit-identical). Side-effect: EventLog.encode/decode round-trips the seed and events exactly. Chaos test: run the same log at 30 FPS and 144 FPS and assert the final state hashes are equal, proving frame-rate independence.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Clamping dt reduces but does not remove frame-rate dependence — the number and size of integration steps still differ per machine — so shared simulations keep diverging without a fixed timestep and input log.

title: Implement view-dependent texture streaming for a 3D museum tour to cut VRAM by 70%
business_context: A virtual museum streams ultra-high-res artwork onto 3D wall planes. All textures load at full 8K resolution on scene entry, consuming 3.1GB of VRAM and crashing on 4GB GPUs (a large share of school Chromebooks used for field trips). Schools are the biggest customer segment and the crash makes the product unusable for them.
constraints: Cap VRAM at 1GB while preserving full detail when a viewer approaches art
Stream mip levels based on the art's screen-space size
Smooth detail-in (no visible blur->sharp pop)
Support 200 artworks per gallery
Works on WebGL2 (no bindless textures)

before_state.description: Every artwork's full 8K texture is loaded on entry.

issues: Loads all 200 artworks at 8K regardless of visibility -> 3.1GB VRAM
Crashes on 4GB GPUs (school Chromebooks)
No relation between texture detail and on-screen size
No unloading when the viewer walks away from an artwork

reasoning: Naive approach: load everything at a fixed lower resolution (2K). Rejected — it fits memory but loses detail when a viewer walks up to inspect brushwork, which is the entire point of a museum tour. Alternative: a single mega-texture atlas with virtual texturing. Rejected as too complex for WebGL2 without bindless and risky for v1. Chosen path: per-artwork mip-pyramid streaming where the desired mip level is computed from screen-space size, higher mips are fetched on approach and lower mips evicted under an LRU memory cap, with a brief crossfade for smooth detail-in. Cross-layer trace: each frame computes each artwork's projected size -> a desired mip level is chosen (closer/bigger -> higher detail) -> the streamer fetches the needed mip tile if absent and uploads it -> an LRU under the 1GB cap evicts mips for distant art -> a crossfade blends old->new mip to avoid a pop -> 200 artworks share the budget -> tests assert mip selection and cap adherence.

after_state.description: Screen-size-driven mip streaming with LRU eviction under a VRAM cap and crossfaded detail-in.

files.purpose: Streams the needed mip per artwork and evicts least-recently-used mips to stay under the VRAM cap.
Computes the appropriate mip level from how large the artwork appears on screen, so detail tracks the viewer.
Crossfades between mip levels so increasing detail appears smoothly instead of popping.

migration_strategy: Add streamer behind flag tex_stream=off; default full-8K load on capable GPUs only
Canary on school Chromebooks; verify no crash and VRAM under 1GB
Tune mip-selection thresholds so approach reaches full detail by ~1m
Enable globally; keep full-load path for high-VRAM kiosks
Remove full-load path once cap adherence is confirmed everywhere
testing_strategy: Fixture: 200 artworks with known full resolutions and a camera near one of them. Assert: the near artwork resolves to mip level 0 (full detail) while distant ones use high mip levels; total streamed bytes stays <= 1GB. Side-effect: walking away evicts the near artwork's level-0 mip (LRU) and frees its bytes. Chaos test: rapidly approach and retreat from an artwork 50 times and assert no VRAM growth and the crossfade never leaves a torn (half-loaded) texture visible.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Loading everything at a fixed 2K fits memory but destroys the close-up brushwork detail that justifies a virtual museum, so it trades a crash for a useless experience — only screen-size-driven streaming keeps both.

title: Fix non-deterministic transparent particle ordering in a 3D fireworks ad creative with depth pre-sort
business_context: An ad-tech platform renders interactive 3D fireworks creatives. Transparent additive particles render in spawn order, so when the camera orbits, near sparks sometimes get occluded by far ones, producing visible dark halos that QA rejects. Each rejected creative delays a campaign launch; the team missed two ad-flight deadlines last quarter over this.
constraints: Correct additive blending appearance from any camera angle
Up to 20k transparent particles
Sort cost must fit a 16ms frame on mobile
No flicker when particles cross depth order
Deterministic for screenshot-based QA approval

before_state.description: Additive particles draw in spawn order; depth ordering is ignored.

issues: Additive blend with depthMask off but no sorting -> wrong layering on orbit
Dark halos where far particles overwrite near ones
Spawn-order draw is non-deterministic relative to camera
Per-particle draw calls (20k) also too slow on mobile

reasoning: Naive approach: enable depth testing so near particles win. Rejected — additive transparency needs all fragments to contribute; depth-testing discards valid contributions and changes the look, and additive does not actually need back-to-front for color but the QA artifacts come from a mix of additive cores and alpha-blended smoke that does. Alternative: full CPU sort of 20k particles every frame by camera distance. Rejected on mobile where a 20k sort per frame is ~8ms and spikes. Chosen path: a GPU-friendly approximate radix/bucket sort by quantized depth, combined with separating the additive core (order-independent) from the alpha smoke (sorted), and instanced draws to cut call count. Cross-layer trace: compute each particle's camera-space depth -> bucket particles into N depth bins (radix-style, near-linear) rather than a comparison sort -> draw alpha smoke back-to-front by bin while additive cores draw unsorted (order-independent) -> all via one instanced draw per material -> determinism from stable bin assignment -> tests assert bin ordering and no dark halos across angles.

after_state.description: Bucket-sorted alpha smoke + order-independent additive cores, drawn instanced; deterministic and halo-free.

files.purpose: Sorts particles into depth bins in near-linear time so transparent smoke draws back-to-front without an O(n log n) sort.
Draws sorted alpha smoke then order-independent additive cores, each as a single instanced call.
Cuts 20k draw calls to one per material and applies the depth order to the instance buffer.

migration_strategy: Add bucket sort + split draw behind flag fx_sort=off
Validate on a fixed orbit that dark halos are gone via screenshot diff
Canary on mobile creatives; measure sort time within the 16ms budget
Enable for all creatives; keep spawn-order path for non-transparent fx
Remove spawn-order path after QA approval
testing_strategy: Fixture: 5k smoke particles at known depths plus additive cores. Assert: bucketByDepth produces a back-to-front order (each successive index has >= camera depth) and rendering shows no dark halo at 8 orbit angles (screenshot diff within tolerance). Side-effect: only one instanced draw per material (call count == 2). Chaos test: collapse all particles to the same depth and assert the bucket sort handles a zero span without dividing by zero and still draws correctly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Enabling depth testing discards valid transparent fragment contributions and changes the intended additive look, while a full per-frame comparison sort of 20k particles spikes past the mobile frame budget — only bucket sorting plus splitting additive from alpha both fixes the halos and fits the budget.

title: Offload mesh decimation to a WASM worker for a 3D scan-to-web pipeline
business_context: A dental SaaS uploads intraoral 3D scans (2-4M triangles) for the browser viewer. Decimating to a web-friendly 200k triangles runs in pure JS on the main thread and takes 11s, freezing the upload UI and timing out on slower clinic PCs. Clinics process 50+ scans/day; the freeze stacks into real workflow delay and dropped uploads.
constraints: Decimate 4M -> 200k triangles without freezing the UI
Preserve surface features critical for diagnosis (margin lines)
Run within 4s on a clinic-grade PC
Deterministic output for the same scan (audit reproducibility)
Memory under 500MB during decimation

before_state.description: Quadric error decimation runs in JS on the main thread.

issues: Runs on the main thread -> 11s UI freeze and upload timeouts
Pure JS QEM is slow; the priority queue churn dominates
No feature preservation, so diagnostic margin lines get smoothed away
Large intermediate structures spike memory

reasoning: Naive approach: move the JS decimation into a Web Worker as-is. Rejected — it unfreezes the UI but the JS QEM is still ~11s, missing the 4s budget and risking upload session timeouts. Alternative: server-side decimation. Rejected because scans are PHI and clinics require local processing for HIPAA scope reduction. Chosen path: compile a fast C++ QEM decimator (e.g. meshoptimizer's simplifier) to WASM and run it in a worker over a transferable buffer, with feature-weight preservation for margin lines and a fixed traversal order for determinism. Cross-layer trace: upload hands the scan buffer to a worker -> the WASM simplifier runs QEM with per-vertex feature weights so margin lines are preserved -> it executes in linear-ish time hitting the 4s budget -> the decimated buffers transfer back zero-copy -> a fixed seed/order makes output reproducible for audit -> the main thread stays responsive -> tests assert triangle count, feature retention, and determinism.

after_state.description: WASM QEM decimation in a worker with feature weighting, deterministic and within budget.

files.purpose: Runs the WASM QEM simplifier off-thread with feature weights and transfers results zero-copy.
Provides a non-blocking promise API that offloads decimation to the WASM worker.
Computes per-vertex feature weights so diagnostically important high-curvature edges survive decimation.

migration_strategy: Ship WASM worker decimation behind flag wasm_decimate=off
Validate triangle count and that margin-line vertices are retained vs the JS output
Canary on clinic PCs; measure decimation time and UI responsiveness
Enable globally; keep JS path as a fallback if WASM init fails
Remove the JS main-thread path after a clean rollout
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 1M-triangle scan with labeled margin-line vertices and a curvature map. Assert: output has ~200k triangles and >95% of margin-line vertices are preserved; the same input produces byte-identical output across two runs (determinism). Side-effect: the main thread records no long task during decimation (work is in the worker). Chaos test: make the WASM init fail and assert the pipeline falls back to JS (or surfaces a clear error) without leaving the upload stuck.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Moving the same JS QEM to a worker only hides the freeze; the algorithm is still ~11s, so uploads still time out and margin lines are still smoothed away — the WASM simplifier with feature weighting is required to hit budget and preserve diagnostics.

title: Add SSAO with a half-res blur pass to a 3D archviz viewer without tanking mobile FPS
business_context: A real-estate archviz viewer needs ambient occlusion so room corners and furniture contacts read as grounded; without it, flat shading makes rooms look like cardboard and agents say listings look 'cheap'. A naive full-resolution SSAO was prototyped but dropped mobile to 18 FPS. The product needs SSAO on by default across devices for the premium tier.
constraints: Visible contact shadows/AO in corners and under furniture
Maintain 50+ FPS on a 2021 mid-range phone
Temporally stable (no flickering AO under camera motion)
Works with the deferred-ish lighting already in place
No AO halos around object silhouettes

before_state.description: Full-resolution SSAO with many samples runs every frame, too slow on mobile.

issues: Full-resolution + 32 samples is ~12ms on mobile
No blur, so the raw AO is noisy and flickers
No silhouette handling -> dark halos around objects
Recomputed at native resolution every frame regardless of need

reasoning: Naive approach: just reduce sample count to 8. Rejected — fewer samples make the AO noisier and flicker more without enough perf gain, and corners under-occlude. Alternative: bake AO into lightmaps offline. Rejected because furniture is user-movable in the configurator, so baked AO goes stale on every move. Chosen path: compute SSAO at half resolution with a randomized kernel, run a depth-aware bilateral blur to denoise and prevent halos, then upsample, with temporal accumulation for stability. Cross-layer trace: the depth/normal buffers feed a half-res SSAO pass (1/4 the pixels) -> a depth-aware bilateral blur denoises while respecting edges (no bleed across silhouettes) -> the result is upsampled and composited into lighting -> a small temporal accumulation reduces flicker under motion -> half-res cuts the cost ~4x to fit mobile -> tests assert AO presence in corners and no halos.

after_state.description: Half-resolution SSAO with depth-aware bilateral blur, upsample, and temporal accumulation.

files.purpose: Computes SSAO at half resolution and denoises with a depth-aware blur to cut cost ~4x while staying clean.
Depth-aware bilateral blur that denoises the half-res AO without bleeding across object silhouettes (no halos).
Reduces SSAO flicker under camera motion via temporal accumulation of the AO signal.

migration_strategy: Add half-res SSAO behind flag ssao=off; default no AO
Canary on mobile; verify 50+ FPS and visible corner occlusion
Tune blur strength and temporal alpha to remove flicker and halos
Enable by default for the premium tier
Keep an off switch for low-end devices via capability detection
testing_strategy: Fixture: a room scene with a box on a floor (known contact area). Assert: rendered AO is darker in the box-floor contact and room corners than open floor, and there is no bright halo ring around the box silhouette (sample silhouette pixels). Side-effect: SSAO render targets are half the canvas dimensions (pixel count == 1/4). Chaos test: orbit the camera and assert frame-to-frame AO variance at a static corner stays below a flicker threshold thanks to temporal accumulation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cutting samples to 8 at full resolution makes the AO noisier and flickerier while barely saving time, and offline-baked AO goes stale the moment a user moves furniture — half-res SSAO with a depth-aware blur is what delivers clean, dynamic AO within the mobile budget.
